speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Jean Wilson Ringsage and Stiner Ringsage took place at their home in Park on October 19th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She was living in Vancouver, DC. She was fortunate enough to still have a job. She was managing in another hotel. She had no idea what it was like on the prairie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not how rough it was?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No idea. I never told her the truth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? You didn't confide in her just how rough it was?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Not until after the fact. No. No, you don't. You don't have any more burden. We weren't dying. We weren't starving to death.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Exactly.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We certainly had some balanced diet. But then, I suppose I didn't confide in her. I don't think my kids confide in me altogether. They tell me the nice things and some of the rough things when their cards fall apart. It's not too rough, really.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But, no, I don't think it... It doesn't mean that... It depends. When I found my youngest girl was... Well, I didn't... She came to visit me and I finally told her then the problem about Laura, my oldest girl, that she was practically blind in one eye, had a cataract and she was a premature baby, you see, and had an evan operation. Well, I told her then that... I don't know. You just kind of don't know how old you have to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the differences that you were having then at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I mean, you say between you and your mother, was it that she... My mother was an eternal... She liked my husband. You know, I thought I was going to get away from Mother's April and spring when I married Diane. I married the eternal optimist. Liked my mother. She was always looking ahead. Things were... Just the unthinkable Molly Brown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that spirit?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Nothing ever sunk her. Nothing ever really. She'd always look at the bright side. She'd always say, this isn't going to last. And she always seemed to manage. Things meshed in so that she came out and she was on top. He isn't quite so good as she was because... I don't know. I don't know. I'm not too sure about him but she was a pretty devout woman in her way. She was unorthodox but she was a true believer. So, you called the Ma of Decade. It was. Well, she was. That beautiful. The days... Well, it was in the 1890s, you know. It was very genteel. People were polite and courteous. And you went to visit a lady and you left... Mother left calling cards, you know. It was the correct thing. And you... I don't know you didn't intrude and yet you were there with sympathy and help. And you had high ideals. And as I say, even the town drunk had his principles.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And the lady... Our little ladies, well, they had their principles too. They were just like gun smoke and they had their hearts of gold amongst those. They were round. They were beautiful. And we passed them in the street. And at five years old, it's ten and eight. They didn't fill in the details but you realize they lived a life apart from yours. But they were... I don't know. They just... I don't think the bubble started to break till after World War I. And then it just went down fast. But it was Horatia Algiers. You were always going to... The good guy won out. Lady was virtuous and so on. She grew up to the ripe old age and her children rose up and called her, you know, gave her honor and so on.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: What do you think, You passed from 28 and went to the 34.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Six years. And you weren't dying but you were just forever wanting things, needing things. I went out and talked for a year but they didn't know when I applied that I was married. I hadn't lied. I just simply wrote my name, Jean Ringsage. And they were... They wouldn't have took me. They said in spite of my qualifications. Because I was married, there was so many married. Me and them meeting, but they had me and they were stuck with me and I'd signed the contract so I went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far away was that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Two hundred miles away. I had to get my children, left them with a... Boarded them out and then for the last six months or five months I had a woman come in and take care. So they stayed at Plaxkill. Yes, they stayed with Steiner. And but, you know, and you're not the only... You're just surrounded by people poor. People have been thrifty and some of them even affluent. And then you're suddenly just leveled down to nothing. And up there, there isn't the fruit. There isn't the... There's just a few native berries. One, in fact, one main native berry. There's just those few things to sustain you. A garden is only something brief that lasts for, at the best, six weeks at its peak. Maybe that is two months.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Good luck.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You have so few resources. Now, in the early part of the Depression, somebody in British Columbia, the farmers themselves got together. They loaded boxcars full of apples and shipped them to the prairie provinces. We would go there with gunny sacks and fill maybe two or three is all we could afford up for a dollar a sack. We could manage that. But if you bought them in the stores, we just didn't. We never saw an apple all the rest of the time. My mother did realize we were struggling. I guess she got it through papers because she did send money. I did buy maybe one box of apples and a box of Japanese oranges and a few things like that. You call them mandarins here. Does that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, is that what pricked your bubble? Is that what made you become a realist instead of an idealist?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, that was a great... Oh, that definitely shaped me. Oh, definitely shaped things up and made you see that this is black and white and what hunger is and how close to the edge of it you are. That's what you're saying here. Things in your world are black and white and gray instead of... with some red. Well, it's sacrifice. Sacrifice and people dying because perhaps they couldn't get to a hospital or a miscarriage. It's been an abortion brought on and die from it. That's the lives that are lost. People that's neglected couldn't get in. The latter part, the last few years of our life, we had a hospital, a community hospital up there for a dollar a day. And you paid... from your tax money, you paid about two dollars a quarter section, which was very reasonable. When I had my last baby, I went to a hospital. At first, there was no hospital. I had to have them at home. In my healthiest state, there was no great hardship. Where they had a section. Yes, but that was the way it... that is... but then you see, there were several years of depression. You just struggle along. Sometimes people would die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People up there that you knew died?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I knew there was serious... no, I don't... I shouldn't say they died. I've heard of some of them. I mean, they were... look, you can die of blood poisoning. You can die of TB. Now, here's one I knew died out of TB because they never got help. They never got to a... there was no one to... There was a wife at home and two little children. He was an Englishman, an improvident Englishman, and he just struggled along and stayed and did what he could. Stayed in the house, coughing his head off. He should have been in a sanit... in a sanatorium. And she should have had municipal help, but there just wasn't. You just didn't get any help. There was no hand. Those people don't realize what a world is. Neighbors would do all they could. The neighbors would do what they could, but they reached... there was nothing. They were sharing their rags like I was. You were exchanging perhaps meat for potatoes if you happened to have a few. It was barter. You were suddenly reduced to barter. Now, it hasn't always been like this in our lives on the farms. That period I told you we were eating carrots and potatoes and bread, but we got skim milk. Our cows went dry. We weren't dairy farmers. You had wheat farms there and that was it. Wheat notes and you didn't go in for all these side issues. You just... I never set foot in the barn until I came down here because we never shipped cream or anything like that. And you had... I had to get skim milk to keep the kids going. But it only lasted maybe two or three months and then we got a few eggs. And I don't know. Maybe we got... I don't know what... Maybe we were able... Maybe one of our animals got big enough we could sell it for $16 and buy a little bit of something else with it to vary your diet. Well I think after I was married we had plenty eggs, dear, but they were so cheap. Yes, dear, but there was sometimes... There was one or two times in the middle of our life when we got going, but there was times before we got going good. We didn't even have that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I must keep on going here. I think another thing that's... There's not only... Supposing you're out and if you're doing well, if you see a goal, the end in sight, if you see the end is in sight and you're going to make good, you're going to overcome something, you're going to accomplish something, you don't mind the hardship so bad. But when it goes on for years and there's no end in sight. Our biggest cheer was Roosevelt down here. We had a guy like Herbert Hoover up there until the end of time, until I left. Just another fellow, same mentality. This man down here gave us a song of cheer. That's when your dad, his dad died. I was so willing to move down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because of Roosevelt?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, definitely. Because he was doing something for his people and one of his brothers wrote that he was on PWA and they were getting some food, surplus foods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More than $40 a month?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And they were getting... Yes, and they got surplus foods. I remember they got grapefruit in the middle of wintertime. Oh, the luxury of it. I hadn't seen grapefruit for years. Oranges, peanut butter, all those things to me. Those were just pure heaven. We didn't get them. We have never gone on welfare. Because we were immigrants in here, we didn't dare go on welfare. Of course, we didn't need it. I mean, we thought we were doing fine.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You see, I was American and didn't take out my papers until just before I was 11 years old. And the reason I took them out was to prove up on the homestead and get some money out of that to move out of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to give up your American citizenship to do that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, sure.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then you have to turn right around and get them again. And that would be easy. We asked all kinds of questions and everything, but you know, I wasn't good at helping. And I know one day he gave me a chart to look at three big letters and I said, you know, give me the smallest you got to start with that. And he did. And he left it there. And I could see it without flashing. So in other words, you had to give up your American citizenship so you could prove up on a homestead so you could sell it to come back down here. I could do all the... That was one quarter. That was an extra. Well, it doesn't make any difference, a quarter or a half or anything. I know, dear, but it was one. We had a quarter. Yeah, I bought a quarter. You bought a quarter and then your homestead was full. That is, I worked three years and he gave me that quarter. That was it. That was the building zone. And that was a nice building. And up there, it was warm and two bedroom houses. I've got a picture of it in a book there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we could show them that, couldn't we, sometimes?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But then you had to prove up to sell that homestead. You had to give up your citizenship. Anybody that comes... Look, you've got all these people coming from all over Europe. The requirement is you must be a Canadian citizen in order to get a home. A British subject. A British subject. You've got to be a British subject. Well, you'd have to do the same here. I'm sure they give this kind of... But it's just too bad that you had to give up your citizenship here and then just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were planning at that time to come back down here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I... Well, no, his dad had... We just got that the fall and your dad died in April, or May. It was about five months before he came down. No, we were planning on living there, staying there. But then his dad died and then... So we had to come down. To get away from the cold and the food. I knew there was more food here because they could graze fruit and they had a longer growing season. All those things were big factors to me. But you seem in this letter to be very worldly despite your isolation in this letter to you. Well, that's not... You seem to be very much in tune with not only events and whatnot, but with the feeling of... I think the feeling of bleakness and despair that you were experiencing was not so different from what a lot of people were experiencing all over the world. Well, yes, we were part of it. We knew we were part of it. But as I said before, we had a newspaper, a good -sized, husky newspaper, like the equivalent, perhaps, of the Portland Oregonian, or more like the journal. That was what we got of Meddington. And we read quite a bit. We passed the loan books around. Mother had a library that would send books there from the British Columbia to me for free. They were non -fictional books, but they were very good, mostly on archaeology and, oh, I don't know, different things like that. And it came somewhere on the card. But then we were... And when you were in politics there, there was just an abundance of propaganda, whatever going around. You could read it and reject it or whatever, but it was there. But Mother, her letters were just as informative as mine. She could write fast and rapidly and she could write under any circumstances. She traveled by bus and she never wasted a moment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she trying to talk to you about losing your idealism?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Is that part of this... I think that's more likely what she was trying to tell me. I think that may have been part of it. And I was perhaps complaining about...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think it changed an awful lot in Canada while we were up there. I was up there for 20 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you imagine?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: 20 years. And I know Dad said you ought to stay a whole year up there. He said you can't believe that it gets 50 below. I said they tell me it gets 50 below up here. And he said you can't believe that. You better stay all year. And so about December 20th, it was quick thrashing. And I went into my pal, my partner, and he wanted me to go with him that winter and go trapping on the Rocky Mountains. And his dad had taken a home stay up there and he wanted me to stay up there with him while his dad went out to work. Boy, that's a hard time to go out to work in an outsize place. Well, we went there until it was 45 or we were thrashing. 45 or 45.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it thrashed that long?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yeah. And it was getting... so the days were so short. Mostly moonlight, you see. And icicles and everything else going in the sack. But it was frozen so hard it didn't destroy the grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said what are they going to do about this grain in the bin there now?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We've got icicles and everything in there. And all of this. We've got nothing else to do. All winter we just fanned the ice out. See, they know what to do. So I thought, well that's fine. Yeah, this crowd bokeh, he died just a few years ago.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You told me about trapping with him. Yeah.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But one thing... And all Americans up there, now they were all Americans it seemed like, went up there to trap homes and things like that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They had a dream. You know, you could be a hippie in those days and be very, very respectful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now this uncle, remember I told you his shop was destroyed in the Frank slide?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Alright, this is what happened to him. He married my aunt. Well of course he re -established another one in Blairmore about 7 or 8 miles and they were going good. Then he married her. And after a whole 3 or 4 years he got so fed up with tailoring. Now he was a real... He'd apprentice the gongs and become a tailor the hard way. And a lot of the men were apparently affluent there, minors. And they wouldn't have nice suits made from scratch. But he was fed up with all that and he wanted to go homesteading up towards the Peace River. This was in British Columbia. And this was my mother's younger sister, a little frail thing. She had a touch of TB when she was young. You know, everybody that's had any family of any kind, there was always one or two had a touch of it. And they never had any children too. She was too strong. So this handsome Uncle Fred, tailored, brought up in Toronto. Never seen a cow or an one at a distance, a coy distance. Aunt Amy had been brought up in a farm but she never, she didn't milk or do any outside work. There was plenty of boys and men to do it. They tackled and they had to go, oh I suppose 150, 200 miles before the railroad was in and landed up there near Prince George. And had this homestead on this beautiful lake close to an Indian Reservation. And they camped out and they gypsied just all along the way. You know, a big sack of flour, you'd mix the bread or the pancakes or the dough on top of it and up again. And she was just an exquisite housekeeper and cook. And they had this little one room shack. We came to lay down and it was petitioned off with curtain. She could just take a little one room place, much bigger than that front room and make it into a... Oh, was that the sister that you went to live with after your father... Mm -hmm, that's the one. But you see, in those days you could go homesteading. And that's how you could get rid of all these young people that had dreams and ideals and fed up to the teeth with the establishment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you just shipped, you just offered them a homestead out in the boonies?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, the country, yeah, well you see the country did. And of course, I protested. Uncle Fred throwing up his good business. He wasn't poor and he sold it all and took all his little few thousand dollars and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he get fed up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: What do you think was the... The establishment!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did these young people drop out of university and high school and leave their lovely degrees when they got it going for them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, same principle exactly. The great fresh air, hunting, trapping, fishing. The glitter, it couldn't have been... I don't know. The family just quietly wrung their hands. But it was respectable. You see, you could do that and be respectable. The poor kids now, it's a shame. They can't do those gypsy -like things. And after about ten years, the end of World War I, he went back to Toronto for a year or so and settled in Vancouver. Establishment again, quite happy. An apartment in town. And then he got into this dry cleaning. You know, that was just a new thing, using these solvents to clean clothes. He did very well. But that had to get worked out. He was a sister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it for your aunt, do you think?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She... Well, it was pretty rough. And she just... Oh, she suffered from loneliness. No children. And that's why she was so glad to have us. And she used to maybe borrow a neighbor's child from three or four miles away. This woman had four or five and she could spare, you know, rotate the entire place. My uncle was gone on a trapline. She was very lonely. Mother...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To have a child to have her company?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You just company. Just. He says you have no idea.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And she'd lay awake... Well, we got a neighbor here who does the same thing. She gets her... Grandchildren. Grandchildren. Well, now she says they have to go to school because she lost them all. And she's awfully nice. And another thing, she's teaching them things. Teaching them how to do things. Not forcing them to do anything. But teaching them how to do things. You were... You know, like when you were a kid, when I was... Of course, there was plenty of it. You had to carry any wood and all that stuff.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He has never known loneliness like I have. He's always had these brothers and uncles just running out of his ears. He was far better with children when we got married and I had a child than I was. You know, I told you about... Yes, because I had this lonely childhood. And I did have a little baby sister, but my goodness, the mother and I both drooling over her wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he knew how to deal with the kid better than I did?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, far better. Far better. He's an excellent father. He's far better with kids than I am. And the little things that upset me about him, he didn't know what to do with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Alright. In here we had a stove. A fire... A wood stove. There were little grapes in front. You could swing them to one side to get... Just doing little things about slips like this so the air could come in. Elaine was only... Possibly two. She found out she could take these... He used to smoke a pipe then, put little matches in, catch them on fire and all that. I used to roar at her and give her a wallop or two. Oh, I left the little things. That's alright. You wait till Daddy comes in from his chores. Daddy will be with you when you're doing it. All day long she'd be in the wood box. There used to be a big wood box there. And she'd pick all the big tiny bits of wood. And his burnt out matches, you know, you throw them out and throw them in there. Fire no fire people around that time. And she'd have neat little piles for when he got through. Now this went on for two winters, believe it or not. And he'd go in there and slip some of those rocking chairs by the fire and ah... And he'd put them and let them burn up till they'd almost burn her hair. But it must have worked something out of her system. I wouldn't have put up with that but he would. Oh, I don't know how many other things. Forest for instance, um, kneeling on the steps up in Canada, we would go down about, we'd come out of the kitchen and go down about three steps into sort of a porch deal. Where we had our sister and wood and that sort of thing. And Forest was hammering. Alright, he gave him a whole quarters worth of nails to hammer across the steps just for the sheer joy. And then tell him about when he broke the, or maybe we have told you about it, breaking 18 windows when he was a year and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, how'd he do that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, you tell him, Stile. Well, that's pretty good. Yeah, that was all we had in there was 18 windows. But one of those... It was a big long chicken house, longer than this house and they had windows and they could come all about the ground up to here.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Eighteen pages, clear across. They weren't, oh, they were smaller than that. You know, it's so cold up there that we had to have, I dug into the bank first and built the log right into the bank and then put dirt on the sides, 12 feet on the bottom or 13 feet and then come up to the top of the chicken house and up a little there is three feet of dirt, level it off, see. And then that was on the back and on the side and then in front I had built up from the bottom about three feet, see, and then put the windows in. And we wanted to make this chicken house good. So we found out we had to have a space, I think it's 12 inches or something like that, a cheesecloth clear across for air, see, because almost no air in there, see, without it, got air that way. And then on top I put straw. I put first logs across, you know, every four feet. Oh, tell them about the windows here. Well, he wants to know why they had the windows here. So we put the logs across and then we put brush, I put brush on as close as I could get it, and then I put straw on, about 12 big hay racks on top of this. And this was 26 by 20 feet wide and packed it down. And then I put a plank on top of this straw here and put a wheelbar, a big wheelbar, run a wheelbar out and dumped dirt on it and pressed it down and put dirt on it until I got about eight inches of dirt all over the top of that again. It was solid. And then there wasn't much in the windows. All we could get in there was ordinary windows, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was it, just one sash or?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, they were individual sashes put in. I don't know, maybe it got them out of an old house or something. They were little, there was 18. There were like four, I think, to up there. There was 18 windows of paint. Yeah, there were four, I think, to a sort of a panel. Maybe four, and then four, and then four like that. Or either that or there was, I think there was six.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Three sixes is 18, that's the way they came out. The way we had back the dirt up and everything, he could climb up. Now he was only four years old. He wasn't four years. Three and a half then.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Look, he wasn't much older. He might have been three because Mother was...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. And so he climbed up and he got my hammer, and that was a heavy hammer. It weighed a pound, besides the handle, you know. And I thought, gee, he's got a load. Well, he went up there, of course I didn't know he was up there or anything. I was out somewhere working. I never come home only for meals. And as a rule. So, well, here was an awful bad story. He had broken all these windows. With the hammer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: With the hammer. Yeah. He had a metal, though. He wasn't gonna leave. Well, I said, that's terrible. Well, then I said, I didn't have to do it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So, but of course I told him, you never do it again, you know. It's too bad you did it. But don't ever do it again. Daddy could also have it. He sure stank you hard. And, well, he thought like this, if you don't like, I worked hard. I know he worked hard, Dad. And he got it all finished. It was so beautifully done. He took all the little pieces out, you know, when you hit it and it breaks corners and things. He took all the little pieces out and made it nice and clean.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And he never had a cut on them.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, sure not. Anybody else would. But not he and I. We could do it. So he got that all cleaned off and I thought he did a good job. Well, I said seventeen when the other one was cracked, so it wasn't much good. Anyway, I went to town and I got this here cheap stuff, you know. Oh, like plastic, you know. Yeah, put that in. Well, the chickens would pick a hole in that other stuff, cheap stuff. It lasted anyway. But, you know, it was really a good chicken house, now that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't hit it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I didn't. I'm a lawful. Oh, everybody says you want to give them a lick and I said, boy, they got to lick me first. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, did anybody tell him not to do it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I wasn't wrong. I never told him he couldn't do it or didn't, that he shouldn't do it. I said that was his own intention. He just says, by golly, the chickens haven't got air in. He says, I'm going to see if they get air in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why you do it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think so. I don't think that was it at all. No, but you could never know what he thought. But those kids, those tools, were not spoiled.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now, he would never do that again. He is the most responsible, reliable, and obedient kid.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Never.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I mean, but boy, they always found something new.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I never had no trouble with it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

He'd have to have it. And, well, I said, yeah, I'd give it to Grey. I owed him $600 down to the thousand dollars. No, I owed him $1 ,300 to the interest. I hadn't paid it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you gave him the land?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I gave him the land about $35, $36, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For that much money?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The house and everything?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah. Everything.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Barn, chicken house, good chicken house I did. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He gave you $3 ,600? Hank? He gave you $3 ,600?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He gave me, I think, $250, something like that, for the hay I had there. And then I had a sale. So he bought some things on the sale. But for the land, for the half section, he bought the old half section. The homestead. It was a good pasture land. And every voice, after a few years, it's got to be worth a lot of money. Any kind of land up there, you know, like that, there's what, $50 ,000 after eight years after that. Just tell them you want $25 ,000 for a quarter, they just come and hand it to you and go look for the quarter. About eight years after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how much did you get for the land then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I don't think I got anything. I don't know how much I lost.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I lost so many... Here's the problem that you owe so much on that place. By the time you got all the debts paid and everything, you already had about...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: About $1 ,500 to all your own, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that right?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For Walnut Creek?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: This is about $1 ,500. That's all you had left after the debts. We came down here about the time we paid all our debts and that, why, he may have paid you so much, but you see, we owed that man over $1 ,000. $1 ,300. $1 ,300. And then we tried to pay all our bills up before we left out there, so we didn't have hardly anything. That was the problem. We had just swallowed up.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it cost to come down here too.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it's quite a complicated business coming down here. We had to make, oh, four or five trips to see the consul in... Edmonton. Edmonton and all the immigration. It was really, really tough. Oh, it was during the Depression. All they were concerned about was whether you'd be at public charge. That was on their mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you strong? Were you healthy?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And that was it. His stepmother and his single brother had a vouch for us that they'd keep us, that we'd never become public and need public assistance of any kind. So we had to make it when we came down here all by ourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In other words, you couldn't do it because you agreed not to when you came down? Is that why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, that was one of the things. Unfortunately, we know we were able to... we actually, you see, we had that little thousand dollars. You'd be surprised how far it went along. No, we never had to. We got by. We managed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was immigration like? Were they nice to you or not?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Or they got your brothers. That wasn't enough. They had to have a second party. And they had to have so many character references and proof. You had to prove. Prove you were a good character. You had to go to the Royal Canadian Mount Police and you had to go to your different neighbors. It was just... no matter what you had, they wanted another paper. And then trail up there. And we'd have to go up there and spend the night there and come back. It was kind of expensive for us. Mother helped us financially too while we were in that state of moving. She was happy we were moving. And look into your politics and what you believed in. They wanted to know everything. It was really a challenge. We didn't know until practically the last month that we were going to come. Whether they'd accept us or not. Just really tough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did this go on then? About six months?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, from May till October. As soon as we decided we were coming down here, like it would be the first you and your dad died the 22nd of May.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: 35. We worked all that year. You know, you asked me some of my impressions of coming down here. You must remember my father was an American. My mother's aunt, which would come up with a great flourish frequently every year or two, some of them, there's three of them, were Americans. Married to Americans. One was a Civil War's widow. And they were more American than any American that ever lived. We could hardly stand the patriotism sometimes. Born and brought up in Canada. There's no Americans like these adopted Americans. But it was fine. They were fine, good upright women. And then being married to Americans, I mean, it wasn't the contrast like somebody coming from Romania or Norway for the first time. A lot of the sharp edges were taken off. I wanted to come. Anything to get away from the cold and the starvation just looked good down here. Roosevelt looked good. I liked his ideas. His brother wrote. He thought it was just going to be a new world for him. It was. Saved his life. Saved his family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which brother?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Helmer.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then there were surprises when I came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want to sleep, honey? Are you tired?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It wouldn't hurt. Lie down. Go on and lie down in my bed. Lay down across the... I think he's doing that. He hasn't been eating much. He's had this cold.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There were surprises. There's psychological things, adjustments that you didn't plan on, that you never thought about. I never thought about the isolation of snow and trees. I felt far more shut in by the trees here than I did from the openness of the prairie. Although there was a lot of people, but the trees were just oppressive. They were so close, since many of them. That was a feeling. And then the heavy snows. Canada had cold, but very little snow. And snow was never a factor in stopping you from going anyplace. Here it was. That was something that you had to deal with. But it was just like being on a stormy sea and coming into a mill pond. See, we were related to practically everybody in here through marriage. Just solid. In Park. In Park. There'd be cousins married to somebody else if it wasn't too direct. His uncle, his cousins. They were dotted here. There were a few people, I guess, we were in a blood relationship at that time, but I noticed even later they'd married.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was amongst people that were first generation Canadians. That's come over from Europe, or more so British, or even American. I don't know that first generation people have a vitality that the second and third don't. They've been aggressive, they've been positive thinkers, they've been wanting to better themselves or they wouldn't have made the move. And they maybe had a little more money in order to get over here. They had something special about them. And those people were interesting to be around. Some of them were very talkative about their backgrounds, could tell you about their home situations and what they came out of. And I always found it stimulating. In other words, that vitality in the first generation up there was somewhat missing here with the second generation. Completely gone. Beautiful, calm. We talked a lot more. We talked, possibly, I would never say it was malicious gossip. They were friendly and open. But boy, there was conversation about anything and everything and some of it was pure gossip, but it was based on the truth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It wasn't fiction. But here there was a quietness, a cautiousness because of the close relationship and a desire to get along. You know, you get sterility from something like that, from a situation like that. It's the same way in politics. All the interesting things, if you're quiet forever and for the sake of peace, keep quiet about these things. You get to the place where your mind just becomes sterile. You're better to fight these things and be stirring them up and letting them flow through the whole community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's because people were related that made some decisions here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think a lot of it was. They liked one another. There was little petty rivalries and all that, but nothing too moving or too stirring. And the lack of... Well, everybody was solid practically for Roosevelt so that everybody was in agreement. There was no church in here. Of course, there wasn't in Canada. I don't think all the time we lived in Platt, but apart from those two student preachers that came when we were first married, I was never inside a church. We were too far away. But there was just a very... Dullness, it sounds like. It was kind of dullness. It was dullness. Yes, it did. I did find it. But then, on the other hand, there was a lot of pluses. Sometimes I felt like Esau. I sold my birthright for a pot of... a mess of pottage, yes. I thought, boy, I've got all this. It's so much plazinger. I've got such a nice big house. I had water in the house. You had plenty to eat. But, oh, that's dull. And then I thought, well, never mind. Let's see if you can just carry on. You won't live here forever. We're going to get out when the kids get to high school. Let's make the best of it. And the quality of kindness within these women. They were just... the Israelites were just so nice to me. When I come down here, they had... Oh, I don't know how many hundred quarts canned up for me. His stepmother and Ethel Helmer's wife were their four kids. She put up hundreds of quarts. I mean, everybody thought nothing of 800 quarts. I don't think I had quite that many. Maybe I aimed for that when I was on my own. But I guess they must have had four or five hundred quarts at least. And potatoes. And they had a lot of the essentials. Now, in Canada, you may have had the impulse, but it was stifled. You didn't have anything to share. That is one of the things people don't think about. It's as necessary to give and to give freely. And don't look for anything in return. If it comes your way, fine. But it isn't an exchange. It's just as necessary as love or food or shelter to give. And that was just clamped down on so cruelly with depression. I couldn't give things to my mother, my family, maybe a little token, but not the things you want to give. That's what's happening to a lot of people now. There is something going on in this big world. That is one of the casualties of giving. So few people recognize how much it means to some people. And a lot of people, even the scallywags, love to give. Not necessarily to get back, but just makes you feel good. This pie of life, it's just right in you. To me, it's just one of the original urges that's in people. I think people share. Even animals. They may get yourself full, but if there's a surplus, they had a surplus. And people now have a surplus. It's able to go round. We give to other countries. That's what I noticed about the United States, that giving. Not because they had... and it wasn't organized. Organized charity. Something's missing. It's like this Christmas exchange. Something's missing. But this was neighbor to neighbor, friend to friend. Somebody would bring you over some beans and you'd have a little surplus. Just a little happy exchange. Clothes to make over for your kids from somebody else. There wasn't even that to make over in Canada. It's just rare. You've got to dress. There's something to make over. We were... it was just dismal. And then I loved when we got out and got a car. His brother died, this Joe, this delicate brother. We got his car and then you can see America. It's rather... perhaps it's rather sad to say these things about the greatness of America, but I just have always thrilled to the real building. Lovely big machines, biting hunks out of hills and the power. I never saw that up there. They have them now, but they weren't there when I was up there. But they were here when I come down. Not like they are now, but still they were great. And the plumbing that works. That's America. I'm sorry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you came down, did these women just give you all these jars?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I tell you what happened. Brother Joe was single and he'd worked. He bought all the sugar and all the jars and he bought the fruit. And Ethel and stepmother furnished the labor. Yes, Joe, his brother was the single one. So that we'd have plenty of fruit and vegetables. Canned beans and tomatoes and corn and all that. Yes, Joe was the one that put up the money for those things. And stepmother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was waiting for you when you got here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I would just fail to death. I've been suffering, hungry so long. There was a school for my kids to get to without too much effort. The children were on the whole way. They were fairly gentle little children. They were a pretty rough bunch up there in Canada. Yeah, that particular school. Always had been. Let's talk a little bit about those years up there. Because just to be able to compare them with these years, I don't understand them. I think as well as I'd like to. For one thing, you mentioned a school up there being rough. Well, discipline was always a problem. When I taught it was a terrific problem. But not all schools are like that. The other two schools, the kids were just good at school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I'll tell you why. I had five teenage boys. I only had about 14, 15 kids. But I had five teenage boys. And I just had the right herd on them. I never naturally, I wouldn't lay a hand on a kid as big as that. And they liked me. And I was friendly with them. I don't know whether it particularly helped or not. But at noontime, one of the girls played the organ. One of the older, grade 8 girls played the old organ. And I'd dance with the boys, teaching them to dance. And, oh yeah, I got by. But they weren't learning too much. They were just anxious to be at a grade 8 and gone.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And another thing that bothered me up there was a family of five that came. Not enough to eat. And he was the horse trader. Those children were so undernourished. I used to give the youngest one practically all my lunch because I was well fed. I'd bring a little extra milk. I'd take a pint of milk and she'd get practically all of it. They had on their bread lard and a little white sugar. That was their lunch for 90 % of the time. And just before I married them, I asked them if there was anything I could buy from most people to try and help me, help them eat. And they had an old, well it was a nice horse, but it was starving. And before I got the delivery of it for $30, it died. It was starvation. Those things bothered me. And then the teachers that succeeded me, they had problems from first one. The boys eventually came out and then there was, oh some of them were accused of favoritism. And one teacher was married and she favored some of her own children and another family. So I don't know. Laura was 8 but she was so frail from all these operations. I realized she needed somebody to kind of watch over and give a little better background. Never mind for the learning part, but just to be kind to her and to help her along. I think down here the teachers were very, the ones that they started with were very nice. I don't know whether they did any better or as good as for their learning, actual learning that they would have up there. But I just liked it. I really liked the children better. They were gentle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about teaching up there? How did you find teaching for you in this work?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I tell you what was, teaching was going to be something I did to earn me enough money to go through college to be a dietitian. I love, I've always loved teaching or cooking. And to be a dietitian or to teach cooking, that was quite a thing up there. You could either work in a hospital or you could, every school has cooking and shop for boys. And I thought I'd like that and I would have. I'd have been good at that. But when I got in a school, the first school was just a dream, no discipline, no disciplinary problems. I began to see the unfairness. Those children weren't having a chance. Little girls that had to work so hard at home and they were kind of tired and weary. And boys that were just waiting to get out in grade 8, you know, there were maybe 15 men that had been kept out of school so much. I remember one kid. I said, you'll be 15.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you going to school?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, not after 15. Well, I said, let's forget the rest of this work. I said, you're going to have to have mathematics. Let's sit down here and figure out fields and how to measure grain and all these things. Now that's the kind of teacher I was. Give the kid what he's going to need and give it to him fast, never mind about the literature. I said, at least you can read and you can write. And let's get on to the things. And he just poured into it. I don't know what ever happened to the kid, but that was it. And there's been a few other children like that. I could just see their need and see what they were heading for, especially boys. And when you gave them something to prepare them for life like that, knowing they'd never maybe see high school again. But, no, I like to teach certain subjects when you had 8th grades. Well, usually I never had more than 6th. The kind of age groups came out that way. You weren't doing justice to those children. Sure you got by. I happened to be looking for something else and I was reading one of my old inspector's reports. Inspectors came around twice a year.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I know that he gave me good. He graded me good. No, I think great. I think if it wasn't for the busing, I think consolidation has meant a lot for children. One teacher to one subject or to one room. Well, and then if you have more, fine. That's just the bonus. But I wasn't conventional enough. My background. I know how I felt when I was a child. But I've got the moods on. I want to learn something, pour it on. I'll take it and I'll figure it out.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And there was,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: oh I don't know, some things I thought were rather superfluous. There always isn't any curriculum. Rather superfluous. Not too necessary. It sounds like you didn't find it that self fulfilling. No, I wasn't. What I wanted to do was teach. To be teaching a group cooking would have been just up my alley. I'd asked what I'd been happy around. Group of girls or men or whatever. I just would have loved to have taught that. I wasn't so, well, dietitian would have been okay. But you had to have college. I had enough credits for one year of college but I needed three more. Then I got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, was getting married, at the time, was a difficult choice to be giving up your career?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, of course I figured on teaching another year there. They liked me well enough. I could have taught another year but I got pregnant. Sorry about that. That was one of those things. That was one of the things that you had to face. There wasn't any handy pills in those days. Rather primitive. They worked in after a fashion but you usually had a few errors while you were getting the hang of it. But I really figured on teaching. It wasn't that unpleasant. I really could say that teaching some subjects I just really enjoyed it. I liked English. I was a good art teacher. History. I could bring them to life on history because I loved it. So anything you're, well, I don't know whether it's always that way but I was pretty good at it. But I was born sick and I had to do it. And then writing. I was pretty good at teaching writing. On the whole it wasn't bad. Math was rather hard because the children found it hard and it was just uphill work. You mentioned you were in control before and how it was really an underground thing at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get the information that you got from?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Most of it was word of mouth from other women. I never got it from a, I never even asked a doctor. It was from other wives, other women. Some of it was pure fiction and others was authentic. That's how it came about. Actually the clinic didn't tell me anything I didn't know. But it was a handy, it was concise to pass on to other women that maybe didn't know. And also a source to buy your materials from. Your supplies of different kinds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were diaphragms the main kind of perception at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were one but condoms were quite off frequent. Men usually quite often were then.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And I'm just trying to think what the name, the brand name is. Suppositories with, actually it's quinine and cocoa butter is really the base of them. They have them down here too. And some of them even had foam. Those were the main ones. And if you're conscientious and you figure it all out, after you've had a couple you kind of catch on. It amazes me with all these years and with all the wealth of technical advice and doctors. I'm reading what my grandkids have floating around and all these things. They're using some of the quaint old methods we did. They had of course, they had the safe rhythm method. That was one, two, perhaps not as perfect as it is now. But I'm just amazed that in all these years I haven't come up with anything better. Not everybody can take the pill and you shouldn't for two.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You live in Massachusetts. They've had a fight back there tooth and nail to get their information.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these clinics, you said the clinics were sort of underground?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, in Vancouver, that was one of the more advanced places. In Alberta there wasn't any. For instance, in Ontario East, you wouldn't dare have a clinic. Vancouver had a clinic, birth control family planning clinic, where they would help women and fit them and give them advice. You could buy any of these supplies that you needed. That's the one I got my information to Alberta through to pass on to other women. But I just feel kind of sad sometimes that they haven't come up with something a little and haven't perfected it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How broad was the understanding? Most women understand the women in birth control when you were growing up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. You didn't hear much about it until you were about to be married. Then you could either feel justified to ask. It was never discussed amongst, I don't think it was ever discussed with another girlfriend. You waited usually until a few months of marriage and then you went to either a doctor or a married woman and she passed along the information. I heard a lot of doctors were very sympathetic to it. Well, I think mine, my doctor in town was sympathetic, yes. Because he was quite helpful.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Of course, you've got a tremendous, there's a lot of French Canadians up there and a doctor would have to be discreet who he was talking to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In your mother's generation, do you think that the knowledge was very strongly known in her among her?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it certainly couldn't have been. She just happened to, I think she was just plain lucky. Four or three children four years apart. She never seemed to worry about anything like that. She says, I don't know what women worry about. She says, I only had four and I never had a butt in my way. So that is one of the biggest hold ups or hang ups or points of confusion with young women. Even in this generation, you run into a person that would be sterile under any conditions, very difficult to get pregnant and they'll give you some stupid little thing. And you know, scientifically it hasn't got a word of truth in it and those women wouldn't have a child anyhow. I don't know my mother, of course my father being gone so many months in a year, that might have helped, but she said she never had the slightest problem. So I think, and she was 28 when she got married, that's something else again. But I was just wondering the change in attitude between her generation and your generation about these things. I understood that like in the 1800s and even into the late 1800s, there was great reluctance on the part of most women to try anything. They did. It was considered almost a sin if you did anything. Children were a gift from God, of course it was a rural district. There was always something for the children to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a religious base?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it was religious and economic, both. There was an economic sense in having a lot of kids. Definitely, you know, America was largely rural and Canada was largely rural and the more boys you had, the more men you had, the more help you got. There was always more work than a woman could do in a house and a man outside. And children, they were, well, even in his case, those boys, they gave his dad a big boost, just a big boost. And he got up into the very comfortable farming area, bracket as a farmer, through his children's labor. Oh yes, they didn't seem to mind it so much. And even in those days, there was the women, I think there were some simple methods of birth control they resorted to. I don't know how effective they were, but not too different from what they're still using now. Some of it was kind of just folklore. Folklore and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember any of the folk ideas that women have been grounded in fact? Were there things you could drink or that sort of thing?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, no, I don't remember anything like that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The period and the month was considered quite important, even in those times. Well, that's not entirely folklore. No, that isn't, you see, it was based on fact. But then one bright soul, she figured it out, well I don't know how she ever only had two kids. Well, she said, you know, the only times is 14 days after your period. And you should never get pregnant. That's the good old Jewish way of being fruitful and multiplying. That's what you think. Yeah, but you only had two. Well, that's just it, you see. So that was her idea, it was just wrong. Wrong. But others were pretty near the truth. It's surprising. One woman I knew, she says she only had two children, she says I know to the day when that egg passes. She knew then. She could feel it. She says I feel funny or I'm not sick, but she says I can tell when it's passing down. And then she says when I get the all clear feeling in two or three days or whatever, I'm okay. Another fiction, another piece of fiction, by a woman that never had a child, she said always keep your left arm above your head when you're with your husband. Alright, go ahead, take your pig.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Another favorite folklore was douching with potassium permanganate. That's a mild antiseptic. It proved to be pure fiction. Now, I'm afraid the ones that were most reliable are the ones that we're still using today, plus the times. The times are, even quite a few years ago they figured out about... Well, you know, this makes me think too of the question of when girls would learn the facts of life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have to wait until they were back any further than you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They must have known a lot sooner than they were. You, let me see. I must say that you learned an awful lot through reading. Books, just plain books and literature. Even select literature that was as above reproach. I don't say about the facts of life, but I don't know how they could write and yet be so informative and say nothing. Like for instance the Bible. Speaking of Sarah, it had ceased after the manner of woman. Beautifully said, you got the whole thing. And reading the other day about this eggland, this old king. They went in, one of the Jewish men went in to stab him and then locked the door and he went over a balcony and left him there, stabbed him. So, just so, now that literature, I don't care if it's mid -Victorian, it's got expressions or just references in a kid that's half ways bright and gathered. And then there was farms. Look how you could learn from farms. You just learn all the facts of life from farms. Far more than you'd ever, and most, there was rural people. And then there was aunts. Now mother was very, I think she'd have answered me anything I'd asked, but I didn't ask her, but I would talk to her sisters. Amy and Frances, and oh they were just most communicative. Any area of life, married life you wanted to ask, they could tell you. And I asked them quite a bit. Oh, and I was in my teens. Now they were women that were extremely happy in their married life. Just joyous, happy women. That was a very good impression for a young person. None of this weeping and wailing and being the underdog by any means. I happened to just been in a family where there was just joyous, happy, loved women. Loved their husbands. Very healthy.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But I don't know where, there was never anything formal and there was great gaps I'll admit. But until you just about got ready to get married, why you didn't. And then it was neighbor women. Happened to be some of the ones you knew more intimately that filled you in. But there isn't too much to know. Actually the whole thing that, if young people, there's only one thing they have to know, start in with anyhow. There's other things they'll have to work out. If they have a foolproof method of birth control, that's the giant step forward. I didn't have it. That was trial and error for a couple of times.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So, I don't feel it. The gaps that I didn't know, I don't think I could have, I could have had them filled in too efficiently with anybody. It was mostly by other people's experiences and what worked for one doesn't always work for another. I don't know whether I missed, I don't think I missed too much. And then there was books. There was, it wasn't too long after I was married that there was quite a few books I found to married people were available. Some of them are dumb things. Others were quite helpful and quite clinical. So you didn't really like. I had the impression that that was something that was changing. In the late 1800s, that it was something that women found it difficult to discuss. They did. They may have found, now my mother found it difficult. And well, I just knew enough that mother would be, she was mid -Victorian enough to be, she'd tell me honestly to be slightly embarrassed. But it was much easier to talk about it to her sisters and her sisters knew the score just as well as she did. And she knew that they told me, so you just got around. You can't always, you can try to give a child information. That third child of mine, she was a lot younger, she was about 12, 13. Laura and I, the older one, we talked about anything and everything in front of her. But you couldn't pin her down to tell her anything. None of these little heart talks. She just, it was just a shyness, a reticence, there was something no one asked me. But Laura was so open and so easy to talk to and talk back and forth and so on and so on. But it's, but I don't think it's so much in people as it is in literature now. It's so easy and so available. I feel a little sad that they haven't, you still see abortion going on, you still see so many unwanted children being born. I wish they had something a little more foolproof. That's the sad thing to me. Oh, I'm just appalled at the population explosion. Well, you said, you mentioned that when you got married that you really kind of wished you hadn't had your kids. Yes, but whatever we did didn't work. So we had to try out the methods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much difference do you think that that made to your getting on your feet at the beginning?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think it made an, it would have made a lot of difference. That extra twelve hundred or thousand would have made a great deal of difference. Yes, tremendous difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Twelve hundred or thousand what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Dollars that I'd have brought in as a wife, a teacher. And you could have kept teaching even though you were married. At that time, that wasn't the depression yet. The depression hadn't hit. See, we got married in twenty -six and the depression didn't happen for a couple of years later. And the teachers were still very much in demand. There was still a shortage of teachers so I could have taught. Yes, it would have made a great deal of difference. And that's why I feel very sorry for children that come along with times, even now when they're unplanned for. I think that's one of the best things that's ever happened is children just being planned for and come. I think that's great. That's a big advancement, big help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it like when you were first married?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was four years, first years, and you were home and you were both struggling to get well. Well, it wasn't, they weren't the happiest. Those were rather unhappy days. I was just unhappy with everything. When I married, about a month ago, I was pregnant. Just like that. And I was miserable sick during pregnancy, one of these vomiting kind. And then I had her about fourteen months afterwards, I had another and just seen one baby after another. But depression hadn't hit and we had one good crop. Take off your coat, I should have asked you before, you're certainly welcome to have taken it off. And when,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: well, when we got over, just got over and thought we might get hit. Now, remind you, we didn't even have a car then, very few had cars. Just barely getting out of those two babies so close, then the depression hit. So we never had a chance. And I could have put off those children, there's just so many things we might have done. We might have migrated, left right away and got into a more fertile area of the world, either in British Columbia or come back to the United States someplace. I know we even had offers, at least they were offers to me from my husband and I for work down here. And this was before we decided to come from people in California. Yeah, right, yeah. But, see the, by the time Elaine came, I was more philosophic and I had, I'd got it all together better in spite of that. I'd come up with reality. Depression, depression really brought it on, alright this is the cold hard pecks of life. You aren't going to have this, you aren't going to have that. And you've got this, now you start from this. But before I had dreams and visions, a very happy little house. But it was very pleasant for farm wives up there for when times were good. Lots of social life and if you had a nice snug house and coal and food, we could have now enlarged your house, put in maybe a basement, lots of them had running water in their house. I shouldn't say lots, but if you got a little affluent and you were tall handy, you'd get those things. But then it was just wiped out, first one thing, there was just never a chance. I think by the time Elaine came, then that was my, even just before she was born, I taught the year before she was born. Let's see, I taught a year and then I come back and I just got pregnant again and I decided to stay home anyhow. I had a little money for that, put into the pigs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where the pigs came from, for the money you were teaching?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, that's where I got them in my name, you see I could justify having them in my name. Of course, the money too got us out of debt and got us some more better supplies of food. Just went a long ways most days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your dreams at that time when you were a young married wife that you would be staying there and be quite successful?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, that was really it. It really was. Because there was no reason why, there was a lot of successful farmers around there where I knew we were just young and starting up, but there was lots of, and they just went down like tin pins too in the Depression. They all went down. You get 25 cents for your wheat and then maybe a bag of crops on top, this is nothing for anybody, the rich or the poor. Some of them had the advantage, maybe they had better houses to start with before they went down. But they had to give up their cars, couldn't afford gas, everything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was that made you change? Was it growing up or in age?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no. It was, I got with one or two people and I got, it was really a type of religion, sort of a religious experience. And I got a new vision, a new idea of life, more positive and more intimate. And it was, I just simply took a, just reversed, I started to look at myself, myself and God. God is the source in me. You don't pay attention to your husband, politics, the Depression. Every day is you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what can I, what can I see that God has given me this day? What does he want me to do to use it wisely?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'm like the steward or I'm like the, a wise housewife. I was completely absolved from bonds with anything or anybody else. And you just simply are resolved to keep up that relationship as being the dependable one. Not even people, it's dear to you, sure. But you know the minute you take the pressure off depending on a husband or a mother or whatever, and turn to yourself and your inner resources, which were at that time just, the juices were really flowing. And every day I would get up and think about those things, meditate a while. And every day was, it wasn't, it isn't that your material things change. It isn't that your mother -in -law, I didn't happen to have one, but we'll say your mother -in -law stops being mean to you, or incompatible. It isn't that any of those things change. But the miracle is that you change. Your attitude, your insight into that person or your insight into this, it changes and it just doesn't matter anymore. Where at once it just was mattering and it was because of the injustice. You suddenly...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The injustice of what? Of life?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Life or the depression, which was really a cruel, unjust thing. For instance, people butchering little piglets down here, I mean it, and you're hungry. People dumping just carloads of food in the ocean in Vancouver and you're starving. I know you're, you just don't, it just doesn't seem just. There's a lot of things that were unjust and they bothered me. Children going hungry, and when you know there's an abundance in another part of the country. There was never any lack of food anywhere during the Depression. It was lack of the means to buy that food. America was just as productive and Canada had just as many orchards, more fish than she knew what to do with, more cattle. It was there, but we weren't getting it and we were, that isn't justice, and it bothered me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was part of your unhappiness?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It did, it was part of it. It was part of it, and I was certain, and then I was part of society and I was being hungry too. It wasn't only for me, I was hungry. My neighbors are hungry too. My friends in the city are freezing because they have no coal. And pea -cold in Canada is a very sad thing. And it wasn't, it was, well it's a large economic thing and I think I was reasonably right in my diagnosis of it. Proved to be so, but at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the diagnosis that you had?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it was something brought on by, it was contrived by the people in power themselves and they were so slow to see. I think if you, well I was very much in line with all Roosevelt's proposals. All right, it was an artificial answer, but at least for the time being it worked. And it wasn't, when there's nothing being done, he did get, even in those foods, those surplus foods that were flowing to people, things got better. Things got much better down here. Every year we were down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see the source of the problems coming from people making too much profits?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Not altogether. It was,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: well, oh listen I could spend three days telling you I'm sending you, but it's, it is profits. It's people, it's part of its profits. It's people,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: for one thing I think it's, it's lack of distribution of things. People are sometimes in some cases, you can, there's really no pat answer, but in lots of cases people aren't being paid anywhere near enough for what they're producing. And there's exploitation, that's one thing. And paying, in Canada you're always paying an absorbent price for anything you bought. That was another factor. People, it was too, too late and too little. That was another factor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean when it started not coming?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, anything, to get a thing in motion to help you or to help the people, it was always too little and too late. That was another thing. And, oh well, it's a huge subject. I've spent hours and days, but it was an economic thing. But then, but the thing once you, but the thing is, Chris, my background always was and continues to be, God's in charge if you let him and you're alive. And, so it's, and I suddenly turned off, stopped worrying about the whole world and started worrying about myself and my own immediate things. Not that I'm blind, if I can do something, do it. But the change has to come from within everybody before you'll ever get any lasting. Because if you can, alright, you elect a good man. He looks good. He says he talks good. But he has not the moral integrity to hold out when he does get in a place where he can help people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you got?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's why you have to have people that's able to withstand. And it isn't easy. I'm not blaming politicians. They get up, they get so discouraged when you're perhaps trying to do what's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't feel that the problems, the problems didn't disappear at all?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, they didn't. Nothing disappeared at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you just decided you couldn't do anything about them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I decided I can do a great deal, yes, for the people I know and immediately, right now. Watch for your neighbors. It's surprising if you would turn off trying to run the world, let God do a little bit of worrying, and just keep, start looking. You don't do it. It doesn't come one day. But start looking. And part of it is almost like ESP. Right to so and so. Mrs. So and So might like to go with you to town. Give her a ring. Do something for maybe an old neighbor. Maybe I'll take him a loaf of bread. Some old bachelor. Somebody, you know, who really hasn't got, doesn't have much charity, is just thinking.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And above all, don't do it selfishly. If you're going to do it looking for something in exchange, you've lost out. And be loving about it. Be unselfish. And have a purity. Don't be buttering up to someone to get something, maybe with a long distance idea that maybe, well, maybe someday he'll do something, or maybe I can borrow something. You know, you've got to, you have to have more or less purity of integrity when you're working that way. But it gave me freedom. You know, I took that. I got that attitude. I didn't get it myself. I had help. And for my friend. And then I carried on and things were getting better. I patched up one of the, there was someone who had never spoke to me for years and I made the first move and they were just tickled to death. And they were so happy. They had, and it was an irrelevant, they'd been alienated. They just became bitterly opposed to me marrying them. They hadn't spoken to me for years. They didn't live close to me and I wrote. And they were all, they were just overjoyed just for me to make the first move. Then I thought, I was unhappy, I had been intensely unhappy about the school situation. I write, I says, I'll wait. I'll get worried about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Unhappy why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because I didn't think my children, a delicate little girl like Laura, I just wasn't, I don't know why. I didn't, the teacher was rather rough and ready. That's why she was able to manage things. I told you about that at Canadian school. I wasn't too, I didn't look forward to sending them there. And I was going to have to eventually. This is the one that was charged with being the favorite. Yeah, and there was a lot, a few other things. Well, alright. I knew I was needing a lot of things. I just wasn't asking for anything fancy. But I didn't, you don't even pray for them, you just wait. And you're part of the, it's something like Steiner's dad. There's all this life force around you and your needs are known. And whatever should be yours is going to come to you. You have to make it happen though. You have to be there and do your part and be aware and be alert. And then his dad died unexpectedly. There was a way out. I didn't plan that. Most of my, most of the things in my life, I've seen them clearly that they should be done. And then they have, the circumstances come about and I'm aware of them and I'm not afraid to leave the past. I can just junk the past, nothing flat. I have to. Really I can.
