Sure, take what's good out of the past. But don't be ever married to a farm, to a job or anything. Be willing to flow and to go. That's why I'm so happy my daughter was able to live, she's lived over 30 years in Portland. It was far, just way past time. For better or for worse, sometimes you're worse off financially.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the understanding that you came to, was this one friend that helped you understand?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, one time, one friend, one meeting of about eight hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you and she just spend?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it was a man. It was the young man. And he was a preacher but this had nothing to do with his, it was religion but it wasn't what he was in. But I was up there.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And she had one, she was fortunate, she only had it on one eye. I had her up to the specialist and I called him. I mean, he'd been, he'd just boarded with us and I just said hi and asked him how college was going and he said great. He said, oh, something so wonderful has come into my life. A new experience and so from there on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, can I meet you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was staying at the YW, dear old age, I stayed in the YW 50 cents a night. And it was pleasant and they had a restaurant and he was, I don't know, he talked to me six or eight hours. We'd take a break and have more coffee or tea and come back again. And then he gave me some, he wrote to me and only once or twice and gave me some life of journey. You just, some things you only have to happen once and you get the picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, was what he was giving you a teaching that he understood?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, yes, something that had come into his life. A group of people had come into his life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this the same group that you're part of now?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it isn't. This is an advancement, I might say. The other has more or less passed out of existence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it have anything?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, it did. I'm trying to think. The Oxford group it was called at that time. It was groups of people, young college people for the most, oh it could be anybody as far as that concerned, it cut across. But it was, it really started in Oxford I think, in England. And people that were, young people that felt they weren't getting enough out of the established religions. And one man in particular was a leader and, well I mean it had come to him first, that the way to spread your faith is one to another. And then in groups and then in homes. And they met and maybe they'd be terrific in the city and homes and invite other people to a home. But never big rallies. But later on it's disintegrated because it started with one person and it clung to that one person which is always, I can see now and I knew then. But I said there's something better. But it was a good start. It gave me the discipline. It gave me that separation, shall we say, of your own identity. Of your own self, your inner self, an independence that I hadn't had before. I was too much influenced by outside things. Well most people are. I wasn't unique. I mean I was responding. I was looking for help to come from outside. It flows, there is a flowing into you and out.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Like that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But it was a great help. A great help. And then there's nothing down here. I've never met another one before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Someone from the Oxford group?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, well it was just sort of a loose affiliation. And it's gone completely out of existence. I'll tell you. It fell into the, oh it fell into the, not that it's fell by the wayside and become terrible but it's sort of disintegrated as it initially started out. It's became the moral rearmament group during the war time. And then it swung over to a group of young people now that are sent out as singing groups. It's up to the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever seen them or heard them? Do you know what they are?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well that is an offshoot of them. That's about the only twinkle. They've just become, I don't see them, I've gone to them once. Did you, you in your life before that though, you hadn't been around churches and you probably hadn't had it or any religious background. Oh dear, you just don't know me. My mother. I knew you were a kid. Oh yes, I mean mother, you just had Bible every Sunday. Oh Sundays were most, we were Presbyterians, we were never allowed, it doesn't matter where you could drop us in the middle of the Atlantic Ocean there on a rocky island and a Presbyterian and a Scotchman would have these Bobby Burns to read in his Bible till the end of time. Bobby Burns may have been kind of a rape but never mind the Bible but cure all that. No, I just thought with your isolation from the communities. Oh no, well you know we went in the summertime, mother and I could walk down the mountain to church and we did all through the summers, wintertime, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was your father, wasn't he?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well he never got excited, no. He was all full of lots of strong morals and boy and lots of honor and virtue and all that but never mind, just lungs. Of course I can see his point too. Churches in those days were pretty stylish but still they were an influence I think for good in those days possibly. I used to go, I never went much to Sunday school. I can't remember ever going to Sunday school excepting in this one place where I went to school for a few months. But boy I didn't know the mother, that was her balm duty and her delight to teach me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I memorized the Bible every Sunday, I went through the catechisms, what is the chief purpose of man?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Man's chief purpose is to glorify God and join forever. I'll recite your chapter and verse. And all of, I'm glad now I had to memorize. I don't believe in memorizing like that all together but still it's, there's only one magic period where it's easy for you to memorize those golden years when you were a young child. It just comes so easily. So, you know I had plenty, an old mother, she may not have gone to church but she had quite religious women friends and they'd talk and this other lady would pray and they'd go over the Bible chapter and verse and mother would say, and I'd say so and so someplace else and they'd go, oh she was always the devotee Christian although she freelanced. We'd go to anything, I mean we never, Baptist or Anglican or almost anything except in Catholic. We went to a different church, wherever we could learn something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well this understanding that you got, did you consider that day a religious experience for you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well I don't know, I think it was. I can't say there was a definite coming up of a sun but within about a month's time I could just see the change it was making. You do so and so, take, I mean I would take a step in faith and something would, I could see the change in my life and then I could see things happening to me. I was able to forget things, I was able to overlook things. I have progressed beyond tolerance for instance, you know I didn't, I mean it was a period of growth to love tolerance and love, but at least I became tolerant. Then you love a person where there's no need to be tolerant. And you don't have the power to forgive people yourself, you may want to. You can be nice to a person and you can humanly, you know it's possible for a human person to be kindly and tolerant towards someone else. But I think perhaps the first experience I had of something else outside of me was to ask for the ability to forgive someone. It's so rarely, I can't hardly think of anyone I've ever been at enmity with, but it has been one or two that have treated me cruelly and just really unjustly. And when you ask and then it's just there, just instantaneous. And the old feeling never comes back again. So something's gone on, something's going on inside of me. That is one of the biggest things. That and being in a disagreeable situation. The situation doesn't change, but you change your inner thoughts, the whole thing changes inside of you and it's just like you're able to stand it. And then quite often I've found that after a period of standing up something will happen to all those situations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you look at it as a personal relationship with God? Is that what it is essentially?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think so, yes. Yes, it's that essentially. Yes. There's a power out there beyond me.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes I do.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It's bringing these things in. And it just happens a great many times and a great many things. A lot of them are small things, just small things. But I find I'm just gently blocked. Just gently blocked. And I'll find it afterwards, it's a good thing I was. And I've found out too that you don't get too frustrated when those things happen. You accept them. The things you have to guard against is... you can accept a great many things but don't get in a situation where you're being forced to do anything that is not morally right. I think I'd be cut off if I was in a situation like that, if I continued on in it. Whether it would be a job, working for a boss that was facing the public, or whatever, you know what I mean.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'd quit.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because I feel that I wouldn't have... I'd be cut off from my source of energy or whatever you want to call it. It wouldn't happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At this time, what year was this when you met in Edmond?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, it was... I'd say it was December 33.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a low point?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, fairly... yes it was. Because I was up there, yes it was. I was slugging along.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Just in depression, my mother used to help me with money for Laura. She knew about it then.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And... naturally, anytime I took her to a specialist, I think I took her up there many a time and they couldn't even operate because she was so delicate and so weak. And... well, I didn't know whether... I don't think I knew that when I talked to him. I was bringing him over Monday to that specialist. I came up on a Saturday and I kind of thought it's been Sunday in town and go to the doctor Monday. And yes, it was a low ebb.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And... I...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Thought it'd been going on for a year or two. And then, I didn't see any more than six months till... Well, it was a little longer than that. No, I'm wrong. It was more than... it was longer than that. Yes, it was... I'm sorry about my figure. It was a year and about five months because I wasn't pregnant with Elaine. So it was probably 32 and then it was 32. No, it was 33 and then it was 34 Elaine was born. And then 35 when we came down here. Yes, it was 33 December. You see it right on the... And then... Elaine... I got pregnant with Elaine. Very big surprise in January.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: A tremendous surprise.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: All the barricades failed.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: but it didn't upset me like the others. I thought, well, this is maybe the answer to something. It was very placid, very calm about the whole thing.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: waited and waited and waited. And by the year and five months, this answer came. And it was all the time I was carrying here we were poor. Poor, poor. But I didn't... I was... there was a period of development and I'm glad I had it because I could stand the change of coming down here. Because you had a... I felt I had to suppress myself in a sense here. As I told you, you cooperate to the point of sterility. It was... I had to drop all... a lot of my forms of self -expression and exuberance and get myself busy in a little humdrum and busyness the other women did. You know when you're preparing for... for to pass your test and to become a citizen, you're very conscious of all the things that could object to in you. And when we came across... oh, well, you're never too sure. You know, a citizen by... you know, if you become a citizen by applying for it and being accepted, you don't have all the rights of a natural born. They could, you know, even at that time, they could have deported you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you're becoming a citizen?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: When you've become one. There are certain things, for instance, of course, there's the political angle and I think there's... I think outrageous political... I don't know what they call it, moral turpitude, whatever that means, and things that they want. Somebody wanted to get nasty, not that I'm worried about too much about either one, but it's surprising. And you're not too sure, so you just become quite quiet. Your exuberance when you came down, do you really feel that that... I mean, it wouldn't surprise me that it be that way, that you're rather a free spirit up there and coming down there you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That I'd throttle down?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, that would be kind of forced upon you. I can see that because I do feel that sense that in the rural communities that there's a certain conformity and state, this kind of stateness. But that didn't affect you up there. It wasn't there. It wasn't there. You really could be whoever you were. You could, yes. There was this... now for instance, one of my very quiet good friends, she was old enough possibly to be my mother. She was a boor from Africa, brought up beautifully there, private schools in England. Her mother had... I shouldn't say... well, her father was a boor and her mother was an English woman and it was quite a scandal in the family when they two married. You see, that's the sort of time boor were. Well, then she was the offspring. She and her two or three sisters, they were sent to England and beautifully brought up and she married this very nice Englishman from a... It was so big that they remodeled the house and found a room that was a room that nobody ever knew about.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Charming people.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And every little... yuckering, those happy souls. There was a sprinkling of Americans there too. Quite a few Americans.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you consider the kind of person that you were that was changed? I mean, if you were trying to describe yourself, what sort of experience did you have?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, just love of what was going on in the community. Politics, I guess, was the main thing. Politics and... oh, it was such a big thing out there. It was just like election year... I don't know, most years, and all your social events, it was there. It was just like, I was going to say, a rally down there, political rally, or Kansas City or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were outspoken? Down here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, up there, speaking your mind. Oh, you could speak your mind much more freely. And people wouldn't get queer ideas about your hair, or if you'd ever speak up, they'd think, well, she's this, she's that, she's something else, she's something even worse. No, there was much more freedom.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Much.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then just going places. I think the fact that you weren't so penned in with roads and snow and that, you got out all the more in the wintertime, met other people. But I just... you're useful, see, you go to those places, and it's sort of a form of self -flattery. It builds up your ego. So you're useful at those gatherings. You're imaginative, and you help put on things to raise money for different political rallies. And you stage little plays and that, just impromptu things and parties. It's fun. But then, before things, just afterwards, I met that person, I thought, well, look, I'm doing this because there's not much else to do. A lot of it's ego -building. A lot of it's because I'm useful. I mean, there's other people who could do it, but perhaps I've only got two children, I can do it perhaps a little more speedily or easily than they can.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And I didn't think it was doing my whole life any good. Because I got awfully tired when he read those things, and he took it to heart. A lot of it was, I mean, the political talk just rolls off my back, but a lot of it he took to heart more seriously than I did. And I thought, I'm going to swing right at it, and I dropped it just like that. But I could always read the paper, and I mean, it wasn't quite that dead, but I quit being active.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was your activity on behalf of the Reform Party?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, it wasn't that, no, it never went that deep. It was, let me see. Mine was more on the social fringes. Now listen, you've got a very small community, and it's divided. But we're going to put a party on. And the money, usually I wasn't involved with money for, I shouldn't say for so much the political parties themselves, as for community affairs. But some of these things would overlap. Sometimes they'd maybe raise $15 or $20 in order to be divided. The school would need it. And, no, and then I... There were times being so hard, I would think that people were probably wanting reform of, you know, wanting... Oh yes, but then we were divided, see, there was division.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh definitely, definitely. There was kind of a farmer labor party, and then there was the conservatives, like the Goldings, they were more or less what they call the conservatives. And then there was the UFAs, the United Farmers, and then there was this new party that came in, this Eberhard party that actually came in. As I say, the previous guy, I forget what his name was, he was a bombshell, he bombed out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you in line with any of the other men?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Not really. You see, that is another thing. I didn't have a vote. I married an American and I lost my right to vote.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's a nice little custom. Well, if a woman has enough brains to marry a good guy in Canada, especially when there's such a shortage of women, you just better... we're gonna straighten her out. So they just take her vote away, right to vote away from her. I didn't think it was quite fair, no, that's one thing. It's been changed. There's a lot of things. Oh, they've changed so much. They've come a long way, they're using their baby as they say. So I really, so I just really freelanced with them all, and I argued with them all, and I showed them, I was the one that put pins in their balloons, and I would write so often. And I said, now look, if that does so and so, that'll do so and so. Well, it takes her eight months, it would. Not to say I told you so, but kid them along and have fun with it. So really you're saying that the political and the community life were so intertwined. Oh, they were just intertwined. You couldn't, if you got into something, you couldn't say that you were really involved with any great thing. And most of the time the political people didn't come there to raise money, they just came there to listen to you, or to have you listen to them, I mean. Well, that was that area of it. But then down here there was a stress on, really on conformity, of people being more acting, acting, behaving like each other. That's right. She didn't exist up there. No, it didn't exist. We really enjoyed one another for what they were. We didn't try to change them unless we tried to kid them. Kid that guy into getting his wife to a hospital and she didn't make it. She got stuck halfway there and headed in the 55 below zero. But it would have been easier if we'd left them alone. No, enjoy. I don't want a melting pot. I like people where they are. I'm just silly. So they whipped and shaved down here at the Ranger Field. Oh, yes. Like they would be suspicious of you if you were a priest. Oh, definitely. That had just knocked them cold. And besides, I rather enjoyed it because I was having enough to do with. I guess we had about the second best home in there then. We had a pump in the house here. That was really an elegant thing. We had class, boy. Yes, in this house when I come down. That was wonderful. After we'd hauled water in Canada for eight years, this was really great. And I enjoyed, we were fixing up the house then. There was a lot of little things like that when we got out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were up there in those years in the late 20s, most of the time, what did you do? What did you spend your time doing after you were married and not teaching? Was it much you could do at all?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, we had a phone. I used to have a phone. And another thing, I went to the diner a great deal. And for instance, we'd go to, the men seemed to trade work and move back and forth from one another's places considerable at times. If you'd be going over to help a neighbor. Now, it only has to happen a couple times a week, these things, to break your week up. Because I had to work and do things the hard way, you know, worse and worse, make bread and all the big butter and all those things I had to learn. And I wasn't as fast as I should have been being an inexperienced girl. But I learned right away quick. If he's going someplace with a horse, grab your kid, be always ready to go within five minutes. And I wasn't. I never kept him. I'd hop on a water tank. We'd have to get water maybe once a week. I'd visit with a woman for half an hour while he was doing that. Okay, that's something. Over to another neighbor. I got a, you so and so's got something I need. He'd be going over to another neighbor. Maybe he'd be talking to the man for half an hour. He's always loved to talk. And I visited that wife maybe an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you went over to work, could you go for the afternoon?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, yes, and sometimes a whole day. Not always, but maybe a whole day. It depends how well I knew the woman. And then their husbands would come and sometimes their wives would come down. And we did a lot of visiting. I mean, if you get out a couple of times a week, yeah, pretty good. Try to carry on the work you had. And I had two small kids. But I, boy, I'd take my bread along lots of times. If you're making bread, you'd just throw a blanket around it and be in a big pan like that. And I'd put the pan, the blankets over it, dish towel over it, and away we'd go. It was bigger than the other woman's place. Well, it seemed the time wasn't so hard, heavy on my hands. I took a woman's magazine or two. I took the ladies home in general. And it seemed I took another one. The newspaper was pretty pawned with big newspapers. There was an awful lot of reading in it. But you know, I was only married about a year. And I decided to read too much. Taking up too much of my time, too much fantasy, and especially fiction. I just ruthlessly cut off all my subscriptions except into the newspaper. I was just fully on that. I'm not going to live in a dream world. I've always been quite a reader and love to read, and that's it. And then maybe after, oh, I went back to subscription all the time. I was there. But I did get books, fairly worthwhile books from Mother. Most of the archaeology or history, something like that. She'd mail them out in the wintertime, maybe only three or four times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start, when you were first married, was it rough right away?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first year was difficult then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Difficult as all get out. It was financially until we got a crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get a crop the first year?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, we did. But none. But it seemed to me, you see, Ed I guess had left us that winter before, and it was his first crop, and he owed quite a bit. And, oh well, it wasn't as hard a lot of times, but still, it wasn't as good as it should be. We just had to kind of watch things, and then I had a baby to prepare for. And then the next year, I guess possibly a little better year, but I was pregnant then with the next baby.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His first year was 26?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: 26 when we married, and I had my little girl in 27. Then 28, 27, yes 28 March, I had the boy. 28, the whole town. All the business section burnt down. And that made it rough, because all the butchers are a lot of people we used to trade with and run up bills at all summer long. We're gone, just wiped right out. And then the depression started right in. We just seemed to feel it from that time on, and poor crops. I guess it must have been, what was it, 1931 and 1932 I went back to teach. Something like that. But you went to teach during that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a difficult time to be away from home?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yes, they were darling little age, yes. Two and a half and three and a half. They were a lovely age to be away from home. Yes, it was hard, but boy, you get so desperate for money while you accept it. It really was tough. But Steiner of course, he was awfully good. He was good, I knew he'd be good to the kids when I left. Oh, but he was just desperate for money. That's the year, I guess it was that year before I went to teach, we were down to carrots and potatoes and stuff like that. It was just wretched. Steiner and I managed, we were fairly amiable about the only thing we have ever quarreled over is politics, and he's so gullible. Boy, even today, say something, oh boy that's a good guy. He was for McGovern.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I just looked at him and said, how could you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He looked, of course he was against Nixon, so I was so darn disgusted last time I voted for you -know -who. I said, if anybody, I said, you can't be all bad, he's a good baby doctor. But he is, he was the same way out there. I was just going to say, I was from McGovern. Never mind, lots of good people work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you didn't have much choice, did you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, it depends on what you tell them in McGovern. I think you had much of a chance to find him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, he is a nice guy, and when I found out how they lied about him, how they - Did Steve do that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Nothing happened. Nothing happened. But he, at what point do you feel that you started to despair about being able to make it up there, make it in a way that you - Oh, I'd say about, by the time, between 28 and 29, I woke up quick. He was always the optimist. I didn't, no, I gave up right now. I didn't give up, I kept going through the motions, but I knew the jig was up as far as trying to ever get out of debt. I said, you should just -and then of course we pointed, oh, we'll get a good cop next year, the price is bound to go, it can't be on. Just absolutely, old Pollyanna. But, no, I just, right now, I mean, I gave it about, I'd say about one good year, and the depression. We'd had it, I knew it. But he didn't give up. Well, I wonder if that was, you know, if during those couple of years that might have really made you feel pretty low by the time. I mean, once you give up, you don't want to be in a place anymore, you still have to be there.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, but there was no place to go. That's another thing. You don't want to be where you are, but you can't, there's nothing, there's no place to go to.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's the desperate place. Everywhere you think, everywhere you know. There was no work, there was no work in mind, there was no work, people were having a tough out at the coast, mother happened to have a job, but she couldn't support us. And then finally, when we got down here, and there was a few others made a dash for the coast, and they had a few, they had a relative or two to give them a toll home when they got in. There was about three families left where we were, right at the same time. But, I don't think he's ever, I don't think he's ever been too perturbed or too, he never worries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If he's got bread, what more do you want?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He doesn't even need the jug of wine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm, me the old dietitian in me, she's always looking at the minerals, vitamins, this doesn't balance out, where's my protein?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But it, but apart, wars and everything else, nothing has touched me like that did, that bleak period. And it was hanging over down here too, I could realize what Roosevelt was doing for us was more or less artificial, but it was helpful, I was very grateful for it and what he did for the other people. As I say, everybody is a Roosevelt person, which I was too at that time, as a newcomer, and he was the first one I was able to vote for. That's when I got our papers. And, but I sometimes wonder if we hadn't had a war, what would have, if we would have ever worked itself out into a period of self -sufficiency without being artificially propped up. That's why, these promises, promises now, I just, these politicians, I wonder, I wonder, I just know they're promising things they can't, they can't fulfill on, not because they are trying to deceive us, but it's just, I just feel it's not going to work out like they figure it will, unless something happens, something, the unexpected. It's rather sad. But I, we'll wait and see. We'll wait for the unexpected. I just wish people had it a little easier, a lot of things. I think, I feel sorry for young, young people, who's trying to get through school on a shoestring, some have it just awfully hard. I think they have it every bit as hard as we ever did in early marriage life. They may be in cities, and they may be on food stamps and everything else, but boy, some of them are struggling. In the miserable places they have to live, and some are better managers, some are smarter about their small resources, I see some of the young people, but oh, I knew a brilliant young fellow out there, one of my granddaughters boyfriends. Oh, what a brain. I mean he had, I think he had five scholarships, three of them, I don't know how many he had he could go on to Reed College, and I think he finished up with Portland State. Oh, the poverty that kid lived in, just a crime. He never got a decent meal half the time. Lived with three or four other kids. Some kind of a miserable place there on, just off Grand Avenue, just a hovel. And he was so sensitive and bright, and oh, he was a nice fellow. He and Jenny drifted apart, but I thought, oh, what a crime to have people, especially with him. I don't know what his IQ was, but it must have been something else, something out of this world. He could speak several languages, and just picked them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the other women out there would, in your way, were they, were some quite a bit more able to manage on than others?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, some people were much more, much more able to manage on, very limited. Yes, there was a difference. Some of them, you know, you'd be surprised, there were so many women in that district that couldn't sew. I was one of the few that was a seamstress. This other American woman, there was about, I think I'd say there was three women amongst us that could sew. They were really good seamstresses. I don't say I was good, but I could make a dress, and I could even make a shirt for my husband. They couldn't sew, and that's an awful handicap. They just didn't seem to, they'd never learned, they never knew how. Just, they'd been in places where they could always buy, and they always could buy before the Depression hit them. They always bought their clothes. They weren't too expensive in those days. And then, suddenly, they couldn't buy them. And there was two or three of us used to exchange sewing for food. I remember I did some work for cabbages. I was very glad to get them. I did sewing for somebody else for potatoes or something like that. We were running short at the end of the spring.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But, some were, well, there's just like now, there's some are miserable cooks, just poorest of poor cooks. But then, there's, others were just real good housewives and good managers. They just seemed to have a few basic ideas of nutrition. Anybody they'd read, they knew how they could put a balanced meal together. But, some just didn't seem to catch on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you felt like you wanted to leave, or that you wished you could do something else, did that, how did that make you feel during that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was depressing. No, just, you read my letter there, it was just black gloom. It is good. Yes, it was. It was just a constant, like a black cloud. Yeah, of course, it was just, I wasn't alone. There were so many others like me. And you just, you couldn't see, and there was just no, you just knew there was no end to it. You were very realistic about conditions up there. You just knew this was it. Another winter coming up of cold, and everybody had a crop of some kind in the garden, but, you know, before spring you'd be short of something every year. And every year your children were getting bigger and fewer clothes, you'd have to have more bought clothes. Mother was buying these little winters, these little snuggies to sleep in, you know, little sleeping suits with the little feet in them, and down here, fleece line to keep kids warm at night. Now, not everybody had them. Some poor kids would be sleeping in their underwear, but she sent enough money to buy them these pajama type sleepers, they called them sleepers, because they had little feet in them and really keep them warm. And helped out that way, and I got Caudley Royal from her to keep them up. Just a few things to supply some of the vitamins. The long winter nights you're short of those vitamins, A, you see, A and D. There's not enough sunshine to get it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend a lot of time worrying about kids getting balanced diet?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I didn't worry. Exactly. Alright, this is it. It isn't right. You know it isn't adequate. There isn't a thing you're, if you can do something, watch out. You know if you can grab a few cabbages, and if you can get so and so, watch your chances, make a break for it and do all you can. You get past worrying. You make a break for it. Well, I mean if you can get some cabbages over there and get them at a reasonable price, buy it quick while you got a nickel on hand or whatever, trade for it. Cabbages was our source of vitamin C. That was one of the main things. You see there was no oranges, no apples. Well, there wouldn't be much, too many apples. No oranges and tomatoes were an emergency. You could use them, but you got to get C somewhere. Well, that's how you did it then. It was really just a day to day kind of struggle to do it. Well, it was more like a week to week because, well, you don't have a store, a little handy, it's more like that. But you're always conscious. There's an end, even in a prison. You can't go on rehashing the same thing and keep your sanity. You have to drop this worry and pick up. You know, they have to get in line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alright?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now there's a day there's something else. You got to be concerned. And you know, they won't have to go to the end of the line. Get in line. And then if they were reasonably healthy, they were healthy. They were very healthy. We never had a, I don't think I ever took a child to a doctor in Canada, excepting Laura. And down here, I think the only things we ever, they had a school nurse that gave shots for whatever. And I think my boy took them in for an ingrown toenail. But the parts were never any sick. And I said, hey Mark. Oh, we got itch. That's right, we got itch. And we had to get some extra medicine in town. It was the end thing here one time. Everybody got itch. The kids had it. We lathered them up and got rid of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Laura's cataract a really serious problem?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Very serious. Five operations. She said seven altogether and she's finally lost her eye. Very serious. And she finally ended up with glaucoma. So bad that it just ruptured the eye. It's a very rare type of glaucoma. And of course she was married by that time. No, she'd lost it from glaucoma. That was right.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Let me see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She had glaucoma when she got married. And then afterwards, that's right, it ruptured and they had to remove it. But that was her last operation. I thought she was a kid. Well, while she was a kid, she had five operations. She had one for the cataract. She had, well, let me see, she had three to correct. Her eyes were terribly crossed because there was no movement in this eye. She had three operations for correction. That's four right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then while she was in her teens, she had to have this operation, something for, what did they do for her for glaucoma?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't know. She had another operation. I think she was fine before she was married.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She got behind in school. She spent one year in Portland in school for blind children. Not that she was blind, but because it gave her eyes a break, more individual attention. And she was happy there. She was about 13, 14.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make her growing up a difficult experience for her?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, it did. She's been much happier now that she's married. Yes, she was unhappy lots of times. And she was small. And it was jealousy. Her little sister was quite attractive. But then, Laura married very well. I think we should call her little sister. She's bigger than her, but she's, everything evened out after a while. But she was happy. The sisters married fairly, although there was quite an age difference. Laura was 22 and she got married and Elaine was almost 18. So it wasn't too long. They were all raising kids together. They were both raising families together. It wasn't too long, I'd say. Elaine's oldest and Laura's youngest were born the same year. So everything's worked out pretty good.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, when we were talking before you mentioned that, when we were talking about women and men, you were saying that women were stronger than men in some ways. In some ways. I don't know why I, when I was, maybe it's my mother as much as anyone else.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, you were perhaps weaker in some ways physically, but then you knew in the long run that you had the endurance, you had the ability to cope with tragedies, calamities. And well, nature just gave you the break when she made you. And, oh, I never felt, I just, we never, I never dreamed of feeling at least inferior. In fact, I thought I had a slight edge, but you might, but you know, as mother says, no bliss of be. If you got it, you just don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But this idea of being stronger, the endurance was more, you think?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We always figured it was, yeah. We must be good gracious. You ought to have seen the work my grandmother had to go through bringing up all those family, even with a hired girl. We always figured the woman had, had a tremendous responsibility. Well, that is the working class that we were, you know, just middle -class farmers, my mother's people back East, fairly just comfortable. But no, the rest, they just, everybody worked and everybody managed and you managed, you planned, you got your kids in college back East there, got them educated to face the world. Well, we always had the feeling that we were the strong ones in any family. Although the man, you like a little coffee. Sure.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Although you think it was more, you can put your coffee in. Well, I'll pour this out. Oh, well, wait a minute. I'll rent it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Get some water in there. Oh, that's okay. Doesn't need to be rinsed. It's just the same thing. Just pour coffee. Well, yeah. It's cold coffee. He emptied it now, dear. Here, here's my cup. There's your cup. You can put the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But still, when you say story, do you think that has to do with like being the cement of the family too?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, that was, it was more the moral, the moral support, the, well, you were the guardian, you might say, of the family's finances, morals, management. Mother did all the business for daddy and wrote out all these letters and boy, you, you had all that. And if you didn't know where the money was going and how it was coming in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You like cream in your coffee?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Just black.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you like a cookie?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, no. So they're right up there, sitting out there looking at you. Oh, it's just soup. Hope your wife feeds you good when you get home. No, I just did. Not a thing, dear. I'll bring two. I'll bring some tea after a while. A mother, I don't know, we just felt we were worth our weight in gold to our husbands. I'm sorry about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why should you be sorry?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'm not saying you would.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that education had much to do with the feeling that women generally were better educated, had more culture than that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, yes. I think the women at that time out west, there was quite a number of women that had fair educations. A lot of them had ventured out like mother. They were just, they were pioneer women. They hadn't married back east. They were pioneers in the sense that some of them were nurses or women that had a brother or something. They'd had education and they came out. Maybe they'd even keep house for a brother or something in those days. It wasn't too frowned upon until he got the married off in the pioneer's days. There was no, not always boarding places and things like that for men to go to. So if he was a bastard, his sister might come for her and she'd naturally get snatched up and nothing flat. I think there was quite a few. Mother's friends were pretty well read, pretty well educated. Not all of them, but some of them what they lacked in books, they were sure good business women. A lot of women. That's why I think women weren't half as frustrated as you think they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because they were the ones behind the scenes in managing their husband's business, whether it was a grocery store or a hotel or maybe some other, managing some other business. Some of them even worked in their husband's offices. Of course, if a man had just a plain job, why, it wasn't quite so. Not all women.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now you take Helmer, he wouldn't let nobody do any of his business. His wife, she didn't touch anything.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's the matter, that's what's the trouble with Helmer and that's why his poor, bright wife, Helmer Ethel, was a real smart woman. Sure she was. Twice the brains that he had. Oh yes. That old steamroller. But you know that's the picture you get, is that the sphere, the women's sphere was limited, restricted. It was restricted. Yeah, it's true. Men had everything and women had something, they had a limited one and that's the... I don't think men's was quite as free as you thought because a lot of them were pretty well pinned down. The support and husbands that had jobs, believe me, they didn't have it all that easy. They had long hours to put in at their stores or their shops or their professions if they had them. Or if they even worked, like one of Mother's friends, he worked, he was, oh I don't know, sort of a clerk or payroll master or something up there in a big mine in this town where we lived in. He worked hard long hours. And you take it... They didn't have much fun for leftover, for beer and skittles. It was just pretty stolid. I don't see that they hadn't had anything too free. So you don't really think he was, you don't think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you saying the men really weren't freer than the women in that sense?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think a lot of the men that had just nine to five jobs, they didn't have it too easy. They worked awfully hard, desperately hard. I don't know whether you'd say the woman was free. She was free to do the house and the church and a few little things like that. She didn't have the freedom women have now. There wasn't nearly the amount of things to go to for outlets. You couldn't go to poetry reading or a lot of things like that, or even an encounter group or something like that. There was nothing like that going on, I'll assure you. But you'd be surprised at the things you can enjoy even within a limited group. A woman, if she wasn't too burdened down with too many children, and a lot of people had a hard girl part -time anyhow, she could visit, she could have pleasant visits with other neighbors. She could go on the train quite cheaply. We did an awful lot of train travel, quite reasonably different places to see. Just smothered a visit with her relatives and friends. We used to go up to Nelson. It seemed to be about a day's journey. Had a brother up there and a grandmother in due time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever come to visit you up there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Once. I was during the Depression. Even then, she visited me twice, I guess.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Twice.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: In Canada. But then the Depression clamped down and she wasn't working as much. She got trained to a different job. She didn't have the money to go. It's a thousand miles from Vancouver. But I came out there with Laura and I saw her quite often. She came a couple of times. But when I was younger, we'd go quite often. Just little jaunts on the train to different places where we'd once lived. And we made the big trip back to Prince Edward Island when I was four. So that was nice.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Mother seemed to have a very pleasant, relaxed life when I was little. She didn't seem too unhappy. As long as Mother could read. As long as she had letters and pleasant people to talk to, amiable women to talk to. She was the most adaptable person. That's what she made me think. That women were really great.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because they were adaptable?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. Any situation. Those are the ones that survive. You could take her from that little mountain home, take her out to that desperate island, bring her into a city. She was just flourishing and nothing flat. Six months time, six months or a year. Here she was. A real well -paid job. Just managing beautifully for several years there before she went back teaching. She could make those. There was no great, no howling balloon, no howling. Just, I'm going to do that. There's no weeping, no tears, no worrying about failing, no worrying about your kids misbehaving. You knew they were going to be good. You knew they'd do that when she was gone. She just knew why she's in there. Give her the thought of both my kids getting out of line if I'm not there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you raised by your parents with a lot of discipline or obedience?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was mostly free. Pretty free. Pretty free. Daddy would growl once in a while, but I can only remember him giving me a swat once. I think he'd swat with me, give me a slap with his hand. I think I was kind of sassy. I think I said something. No doubt I needed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see those buffalo horns up there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: All right, on those two lower buffalo horns, there was always a set of switches. Just sort of more of the switches. Once in a while, when I'd make my mother desperate, she'd give me a few licks across the back with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know what was mostly about?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Clothes. Come every fall, there was a battle. I didn't want long woolen underwear on. It scratched. I hated it. I whipped. I'd roll it up above my knee. And then wool stockings, another battle. The underwear was lumpy. I couldn't make it fold over nice around my ankles. I think I got swatted pretty early every time. And there was other things. I was kind of delectry. I was kind of delectry, getting dressed. Take my own sweet time. She had her broke in when I got her. Her mother and I were just alike. Oh, she thought he was an angel. She thought he was the most wonderful husband. They just sided up and ganged up on me. Oh boy, they were pills. Oh, they were just awful. Right now. No wonder I didn't let my mother visit too much. I never had to say a word. Mother said it all. I got cheated. Her mother would always jump in and say, you just leave him alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they really alike?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were. That's what I say. I got shortchanged when I got married. You know when I dream, I dream quite often of my mother and I dream of him. I'll be talking to him or he'll be under, and then it's mother. And then mother, she'll be saying or arguing, explaining. And then it's him. They're just absolutely twin souls. When mother died, I felt I'd lost one of a pair of oars in my life.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Still got one.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was going to tell you one more thing about discipline. I used to, oh I don't know. They out -talked me. That's one thing. My mother and her sisters, they were very persuasive. Little Kissinger's running around. That's what they were. But I remember I pulled a Gandy when I was only about five years old. I don't know what it was. They wanted me to do something. And I protested. No. It was likely about clothes or putting a coat on before I went outside. I calmly sat down in my larger kitchen, about like this, in the middle of the floor, right in the flow of traffic. I crossed my little legs. I didn't know about Gandy either. He wasn't around. And I just sat there for about an hour, waiting. Nobody paid any attention to me. Finally I got tired and got up. That's the best way to do it. But I knew lots of parents would have just blistered my little bottle. But no. Nobody paid any attention. I had my little Gandy defiance streak there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Gandy call him? His sit -ins?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Nonviolent. Yeah, that's me. That's it. That's what I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that they were so similar?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They both talked a great deal. They're absolutely always optimistic. You can be at the bottom of a coal chute and a load's coming and, oh no, it isn't going to hit me nine times out of ten. It doesn't. Absolutely optimistic about everything. The realist, I don't know. I can't say that it's always consoling. Sometimes it's nice. Oh, they bomb out sometimes. They aren't always right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would make them side together against you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It didn't matter what it was. If my husband would ask for something or want something, if he'd want four loaf of slices of bread, when I figured he shouldn't have it, I'd say, It's not that you're eating too much bread. Eat your vegetables. If you're hungry, fill up with some fruit. Oh, let him have another loaf of bread. He's a hard -working man. You know, just any little thing like that. If he wanted something, he just might ask me gently and I'd say, no, oh, he always, you know, he'd swing right over. Swing right over and help him out and agree with him.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, every time. It was too agreeable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you tried to raise your kids more or less the way she raised you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think so. Although my children were very different from what I was. Very, very different. Elaine, the youngest one, is more like I am. I think it's because she didn't have the other two so close to her. The other two were more interested in themselves and playing with themselves. They didn't talk so much to me or wasn't, they weren't particularly interested in being read too much. They were action. They were like their dad. Action, hard work, moving things, tearing things down outside.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: A lot of people would have gave her a lickin' when she was little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who? Me?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, Elaine. Oh, yeah, well, I don't know. By golly, we'd never give her a lickin' that I, oh, well, you did, yeah. Well, I'd wallop her every morning and pretty well, I'd comb her hair. She had hair in braids. But there was things, like we was missing eggs, we used to have a lot of eggs, you know, and we'd find them and get any. Well, not quite, they were dropped, they dropped down. Playing with the chickens. Yeah. She found out that he didn't like the eggs, so she was breaking them for him. Feeding it to him. I see. I see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It's a soul or something. Yeah, I don't know, I don't think it ever helped. No, that's what they thought. And there's Forrest, look, he just went right there. He's a big officer and never had no trouble. I says, you know, to Forrest, let's see, I'm forgetting what it was.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And he said, well, he said, I just knew I had to get right down on my belly and study. Oh, you know, we tried to get him to go to high schools there in Portland. Sent him to four different ones. Because we couldn't get one of the boys from here to go with him. You know, why, he just couldn't stand a bunch of strangers. He'd be too lonesome. And so, finally, when he got in the service, that's what he wants. I signed him up the first day he was of age. And his mother wouldn't do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His mother didn't like that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, no, but I signed him up. Well, he was already salvaging. I didn't mind going off and getting shot and all that gruesome thing. But his dad says, let him go. There was a war on, you know. And I said, well, listen, a guy that wants to go to that bed, he'll never get hurt. He wants to get in there, there's something that tells him that he'll never get hurt. And he's never been hurt today. He and his dad now live in Franklin. He's been in there all the time.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Stan was just awfully good father with the kids. I mean, he'd just take them out and work with them. They were always together. Very amiable. Very, very... I remember one day... Laura was happy in her own little way. She liked... I'll tell you one thing, I never made my kids. I did a few times. It wasn't working so hard. I used to yell at them to do dishes or do this. But finally, especially when I got to the city, here on the farm, I had a pitch in and the kids helped me. Laura did outside work and I did the inside work. But I found out, that's where I'm more permissive than a lot of mothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All right, what do you want to do?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Nobody wanted to do dishes and I didn't blame them. I didn't like dishes either. You know that's worse, yeah. All right, Laura says she'd... She liked to mow the lawn. Smaller she is, she liked gardening. Elaine liked to wax floors and tidy in dust. All right, I'll do the dishes. No problems. Everybody did what they had to do. Elaine would ball me out for messing up the front room. She says, you're going to be about 13 or 14. She says, Mother, there's just nobody in the world can come home from town and in five minutes occupy every chair and davenal in the front room. Purse, package, coat, hat. She says, there's five things all taken up. All right, dear, I'll pick them up. So I go over and pick them up and cut them away. But I don't know when she turned out. Now, I never...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell your daughters to cook?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No. She's just the best cook. Laura, I did make an effort with her. I sent her to a girls polytechnic school, you know, because she's the lousiest cook in the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, so what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And she doesn't like to cook, she likes me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I was permissive, as you could be, you know what I mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Both choices would work. Anybody can read. I couldn't cook so much when I was married, when I first married, because I never had the chance to learn. I mean, there was always myself and a nam. And she did all the cooking and I'd help. But anybody can read a cookbook and learn to cook. It isn't that hard. Don't make the unit make a big deal out of it. If you like to cook, you'll do it. You'll have to train the kids to do it. That's her one. You'll know me. You can't help it. Two and a half, dear, two and a half. You know, you talk about your father's attitudes about who you should and shouldn't marry. Now, we talk about these lazy Englishmen. Those remittance men, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What remittance? What does that mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, at that time, there was a great many remittance men. They were the scallywag, the scallywag, young sons usually, and fairly well educated of the English gentry, shall we say. That family was so fed up with them, they maybe got in a scrape or two, ran away from the army or got a serving girl in trouble or whatever. So they gave them maybe $30 a month or something to stay out of the country and stay here. And as long as they stayed here, why, they'd get them. So they were called remittance men because they got remittance from the old country. Well, daddy was just fed up to batiste with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there really a lot of them growing around?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, there were. I'm not kidding. Well, I shouldn't say a lot, but every locality might have two or three of them floating around. There was none where we lived and everybody was farming. But that would be about 20 years before, or 30 years before I was off there farming. They'd thinned out. They'd been at war and they'd had to go to war and been honorably shot. But at my time, I mean when I was a young child, they were still around. Daddy would save us going that remittance man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So were most of the Englishmen around there remittance men?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, there were some real good, there were some solid married men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would these men do while they were there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They'd just float around. Maybe they'd take a job up or have some little clerical job that only took a short time of their day. A lot of them had drinking problems.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they didn't contribute too much to the community?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, not really, excepting charm. Yes, quite often they were very charming. I was thinking about education, sex education and things. How you're molded when you're very, very young. Daddy would mention some of these remittance men and snort. And then there'd be maybe words. Well, some of them actually would marry. Well, I don't know who'd put up with him and so on. Mother would quote from Locksley Hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does it say?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Anyhow, when they're married, he will hold thee after marriage. When the love has lost its force or something like that. A little better than his dog, a little dearer than his horse. You know, just about that. That's Locksley Hall. About a typical Englishman that's interested in his horses and his dogs. Once the first bloom of love's over, well, yeah, a little better than his dog and a little dearer than his horse. So in other words, there was a little bit of a... Some of that attitude was not the highest opinion of the English. That's right. They didn't have... Themselves. Themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that partly because the English had such status pretensions?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They did. There was some very pretentious English. They didn't contribute anything, excepting, you might hear, a long list of notables that they had descended from. Or they'd known better days in the old country. And, oh, English didn't seem to have a very good status in our... There was mostly working. The town was built on a coal mine, around a coal mine. You had your clerical workers in the office. Coleman and Blerma, both of them. And then you had the merchants. Now, for the most part, there was a lot of them... The workers were Welsh, there were Bohemians, there were Poles, there was some English, there was some Scotch, but there was a lot of Scotchmen. And Russians. And there was a sprinkling of Russian and those people. And Austrians and things like that. But not too many. Well, there was a few Englishmen, I suppose, that had followed minds in the old country. They were pretty good guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of class distinctions were there at that time? In the small town?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I mean, there generally are. Yes, there is. The distinctions are very much like they have been in the United States. White, Anglo -Saxon and Protestant. They were, whether you were Scotch or Irish, the Northern Irish, some Northern Irish and me, or even English. That was the upper crust. Regardless, I mean, there was no lords or ladies or anything like that. That was the cream of the crop. Then, that was the majority of the people. And your business would determine, too, your status. But they really were the people that held the jobs, the better paying jobs. The people in the mines, they were the ones that, although actually they had more money than some of the others because they worked hard. But they were people that spoke broken English. A lot of them were Catholic.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Just the same distinctions that are here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, going down the status, underneath the Scotch and the Wasps, who would be next?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, the Scotch were in with the Wasps. Yeah, that's what I mean. Next would come these hard -working miners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like the Cornish?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I said Welsh. They were in with the group, too. They were white and Anglo -Saxon and Protestant. That put them in whether they worked in the mines. There was very few. There was maybe the doctor, the mine owner or the superintendent, and a lawyer or two, and a banker or two that were the top. Well, the bartenders, no matter what they were, they were on the fringes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about at Wainwright?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, there was more white Anglo -Saxons there than down in the coal mines. There were far more people that were white Anglo -Saxons. They're practically all farmers, excepting for a small town like Deary or a little bit bigger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the farmers really were the elite?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were the elite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not the townspeople?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, the lawyer and the lawyer, the doctor, the banker, and perhaps the lumberyard keeper, maybe the merchant. The rich farmers would be on a par with the merchant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the democraticness of the people towards each other in a community compared there and here? Was there less or more class distinction up there, do you think?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I couldn't see a great deal of difference. Either place. I think I see the same distinction possibly today, even like in a little place like Troy. The banker, the doctor, the dentist, they're just perhaps a little cut above the average farmer. Some of those farmers could maybe buy them out and sell them again. Oh yeah. But they are because of their training and that. They're respected.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this anti -Catholic feeling up there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It seems like your father certainly had it in his opinion of the Catholics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it come from?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, listen, it's history. Actually, it's history. I know it is. After all, they were the ones that got defeated. We won, I'm sorry. Well, you take any community that put, we always, they were the ones we always felt put a burden. They had these huge families. Some of them, there were the great many of them were poor. We didn't have too many, but there was always a few of them. And eight or ten children. I think possibly they were the ones that got a lot of, if there was a little charity tossed around, they had to have a helping hand.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was a merchant though, I knew he was a French Canadian. He was well respected because he was, he had the, you know, he'd come up the ladder. Money counts in any country. Now, French Canadians got money and a good job and got reasonably honestly. Well, he's acceptable. But it's like the Kennedys, this man had charm and personality and I think, I think it's, I'm sure now it's melted. You know, it's a problem. When I was back in Ontario about five or six years ago, I went to the Montreal Expo with my sister. And, oh, we'd read the papers there. They were just in a frenzy. One small place, I think it was outside one of the big cities. I guess this was up in Toronto at that time. It wasn't Quebec, it was Toronto. It was a whole community was insisting on, I guess they were in the majority of the French. They were insisting on speaking French and having all their children talk French. They refused to speak English. And, well, there was the English, I'd read the letters, we were there for about a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Letters from the Canadians, mothers and parents, what are we going to do with our children? They can't speak French?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And the teachers, oh, you know, you can just imagine how it would be. You're a kid, just like this busting. They'd either have to bust them or to separate the school. Well, most of these kids are French. Oh, yes. I mean, I don't mean, I don't mean... Oh, yes. They're mostly, mostly French. Well, I shouldn't say mostly, there was some of the Bohemians and Polaks. And Catholics. Yes, oh yes. Some of those foreigners were strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the attitude at the time that you got from your parents that there was something wrong with their religion? Or was it something wrong with their culture? Or what was it that was inferior?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well... Not the kind of people you would want to consider marrying, say. Look, if you marry a Catholic, let's see, let's say we start out like this. Let's get out the nitty gritty. Alright, you marry one. My son was almost about to marry a Catholic girl. It wouldn't have mattered if it had been me, it would have been the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you prepared to let your child, you'd have to have your child, especially in those days, be brought up in the Catholic faith? Now, mine's the Catholic Church has come a long way like everything else, but that's what it meant, alright?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I would not be willing. Definitely my parents wouldn't be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Am I prepared, are you prepared to support all the children of God's hand which may be 10 or 12?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's what I said to my son, oh, that's it, she's a beautiful girl, I said, but you know she's an Irish Catholic and she's told us. She leveled with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says, are you prepared to support all the children that's going to come your way without doing one little cop and pick and thing to prevent them coming?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now some Catholics just will not have anything to do with birth control, even the rhythm, some of them are so fanatical. Well, it's things like that. Here you have all these kids, poor maybe, I mean if you're on a modest income, by the time you've got 10 kids, you're a poor person. You're struggling to bring them up, it's hard work, you're tired out, you're dragged out. That's the main thing I got, I don't care if they think the Pope's the greatest in the world, it's the practical nitty gritty things. You're more apt to have to go on welfare and have handouts. You live in a small community, farming community, like in some areas, supposing you're in Quebec, your kids have to go away to make a living. They have to become educated in some trade by the government. Now they do it, but at first they didn't. It's just an uphill battle for them. Most of them come from the poorest of homes, they've had no culture, nothing but church and home. That's in my time, in my generation. Now they're just, the borders and the lines have just smeared out practically. But it was so clear cut. Here you were, would you want to see your daughter like mother, she had three kids. Her sisters didn't have any, but one, one sister has one child and that's it. I wouldn't want to see a daughter of mine, I wouldn't want to see my son supporting all those children. That's what it comes down to, it isn't a religion, it's the result, it's the practical thing, it's the result. And even if you go further, an abortion, no matter how sick that mother is, how bad she is physically, we had a friend, his name is Jeffy, she was an insurance agent for some company out there, and of course he was going to get all he could, and he went to this Catholic family. They thought it would be good to have a modest insurance, just to even bury one or the other. They had to have physicals. The man passed just flying, nothing to it. I think they had 18 kids, they were 17 by then. That poor mother, she had, the man, the doctor says, Jackie says that woman has everything wrong with her in the book and she's still having children. Her muscles were all torn on one side. Pregnancy, she couldn't wear a corset, she was too deformed on, she wasn't a big woman. She just had to take tight sheets, wrap them around of flour sacs, they had lots of flour sacs in those days, tied around. You know in pregnancy, the pain, carrying a child more to one side, she just rubbed that through. And all the other things wrong, they had no more than 20 pounds of sugar in that place. And when I came down here, I saw there was a great piece in the form, and this is the name, some French name, they had received a letter from the Prime Minister of Canada on the birth of their 18th child. And Jack said it just used to make him boil. Every year, another one, the family priest would come and baptize the little darling. Fine boy you got there, fine girl you got there, John boy, he's a lovely child. Another next year, God willing father. Now you see, it really isn't the religion, it's the everyday practice of life, that's what happens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any, do you remember the Ku Klux Klan being all active up there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, no, I don't. Because there were down here, you know. Oh I know, I know, and you know, I never knew they were out west until two years ago, because I don't say I'm a whiz at Canadian, at American history, but you know they were over there in the ground. This, I knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two years ago?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, not two years ago I found out, this happened before the war, the last war. But Mrs. Siegrist was a Catholic of course, she was a French Canadian and came right from Canada, and oh she was a beautiful woman, a lovely old lady at 90 now. And she told me her brother had, he slept in the same rectory or whatever the Catholic priest did to help protect them. They were up there so strong, in Legrand. I said I didn't know they came out of the site. And she says yes, and they were so bitter against us Catholics, she says the priest for a while, they threatened them and threatened them so and said dreadful things about them, that my brother, he wasn't married then, he went over there to just give them a little. That's who they were against, out here at the Catholics, not the actors that were in the West. Well that's what I was so surprised at. I said I didn't know they ever cut me farther than the Mississippi. You used to be out there on the blacks. Yeah, but out in the West. No, I don't think there was anything like that up there, because there was no blacks either. What happened, I don't think they'd make, they wouldn't get anywhere in French, down there in Quebec. I don't see what they, well I think it's purely an American. Well actually I've heard the Greek community too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, there might have been Americans that were doing it, but I've heard they were in Saskatchewan, wherever, that's why I mentioned that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder where they'd be located, what were they fighting against up there? French Canadians?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I just wondered. No, they never got up in our district. We didn't have, we had French Canadians, but they weren't in abundance, they weren't too many. That's why the Catholics are getting more lenient now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used to be I think, don't you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They're stronger politically there, and they're stronger for their own language in Canada.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that the Catholics there tended to be considered more lower class at that time? Yes I do, I'm sorry, it wasn't that they were sorry, but it's like this, it's not, let me see, how could you say it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It's like a Negro in a ghetto. They are so underprivileged from lack of education, and from not, and they're limited, their food supplies are alright, so there's some comfortable ones, but they're so limited. I don't suppose hardly anything gets beyond grade 8, and they just go to church and raise these huge families, they're just so circumscribed by their economics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Ukrainians out there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Prosperous, prosperous.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Ukrainians in your wing right were prosperous in all reality?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Marvelous workers, free souls, they didn't take, they were, now they were, they're, listen, they're Greek Orthodox, they're a form of Catholicism, but their priests can marry once, I think, something like that.
