They are Catholic, but the ones up there, boy, the priests never had a chance for them, of course they wouldn't pay any attention to the Roman Catholic, and there wasn't any Greek Orthodox around, most of them I knew were married in the Protestant churches. And they were free souls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were considered, let's say, in a round wing right, were they considered higher class than the Catholics were?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yes, definitely, and they keep their ethnic customs and their singing, and they're marvelous workers, and they seem to, now you find them advancing. These people we know, their kids have all been well educated, they've married well, good positions, and they're so ambitious for their children, and they're smaller families, they don't have these huge families, they definitely, anything that's, and they're active politically, and they assimilate, and yet they keep their nationalities. They keep their national customs, they have their dances and their fun times together, I like the Eucharina.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they mix much with the other people?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yes. Here comes somebody. They mix with us very, very much. Oh, this is fun. There's something like, you know, very strict authoritarian father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't get along with his father?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Just couldn't get along with his father. Mother told me that much, but it was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he come from in the States?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well listen, he was born in Liverpool, England. I think they were en route to the United States at the time. He was born there, and he was just a, oh I don't know, I don't suppose it was a year old when he came to the United States. And there was about eight brothers and sisters. But I'd love to find my family, but Wilson is such a common name, it's so hard to trade. They settled in Vermont, he had one or two of his schoolbooks, he had his old arithmetic with them. He always hung on to it, yet I don't know where it's vanished. And the place, the name on it, New Hampshire Roads,

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Al and I can't find it on the map. Al is our son -in -law, and he's one of his hobbies, genealogy, family genealogy. We found mother's birth certificate, they were, they're married and he was registered, we know the name of the slaughtering mother now, and we're sort of at a dead end. And Al has advertised in some of these books, but he's never had a nibble on them. They have their publications, these people and dig into family history. So, but that's all I know about him. Really, I know so little I'm ashamed. He had a sister named Emma, he seemed quite, he spoke of her father. He, if I'd ask him anything, he'd just clam right up. Over 50, yes, I'd like to know. But his father was just very, and he was the baby in the family, I'm surprised. And he was a marvelous worker, and he was bright, and good, well -disciplined and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know right away, when, after your father died, did you know right away that this is what you wanted, to come down here and take this place?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it much of a struggle in your mind to decide what you wanted?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I wanted, nobody else wanted it. But my oldest brother, he was always that baby. He wanted my youngest brother to have it, my youngest brother says, I don't want it. See, the youngest brother didn't have a home. And he still tried to force it onto him. That's Helmer. Yeah, that's Helmer. And here, and then they wanted my uncle to be the administrator, see. Well, I had dealings with him up there, and he didn't, he didn't come out just the way I wanted him to. So, we didn't agree on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that up there? Because of selling the horses and that sort of stuff? No, well, I told him, I said, gosh, well, I said, couldn't you let the saddle horse in the saddle?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I said, you know, every day I've always had to wrangle in the horses. We had a bunch of horses, 19 I think it was. Well, with the saddle horse, 19. And they were scattered over, we had a section and a quarter I think it was. And so, he says, well, he said, I'd like to, but he says, that guy says, I'll give you $500 for the horses. And he says, I want everything. Saddle horse, the saddle, bridle, everything. So, it'd be turned down all the way, and there's nobody else up there when you come to think about it that would get five cents for anything. They just didn't have it. They wanted you to walk off and leave it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, what was it then that you didn't agree with him about, on there, for the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The uncle you mean?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. Well, like he told me, when I come out of the mines, you know, we'd make an agreement. He says, you know, I stayed the one year, see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Then, an old guy there in that district, he used to farm up here in S. Purse, Idaho. And my brother used to work for him. I was well acquainted with him. Olson was his name. And he had quite a few binders. He'd been working for a fellow named Myers or some darn thing like that. And he had several hundred acres. And he farmed 450 acres. And he bought a bunch of binders, and you know, he hired these guys to run the binders. And he was a poor farmer himself. If a rock was this high, a big round rock like that, you know, he never thought about clearing that off his field. They'd just throw the grain in there and run the drills and everything over to around it, you know. If the horses could pull it, they'd pull it right over the top of it. That's the way those guys did it then. So, when they got through the binders about two years old, why, the bottom part was shocked. The drapers wouldn't turn. You know, got over there and smashed it underneath there so the drapers wouldn't turn. Well, he didn't know anything about the binder. There was a good binder. I hadn't looked, I knew all about that binder. Or any McCormick binder. I'd take a brand new binder, look at it, and I could tell you whether it was any good or not. I looked at the packers and all that stuff, and it just liked new. See, everything that did the work would like new, only the bottom part was shocked. Well, I said I'd straighten that out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how much do you want for that binder you got in the brush?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, the brush moved around it for a couple of years. Well, he says, take eighty dollars. See, I said, okay, I'll give you eighty dollars in the fall. So, in the fall when I got a little more money, I went over. Well, he said it's eighty -five now. He said, you know, interest fee. I said, okay, I'll give you eighty -five. But I didn't like that exactly either. But, you can't do much about it. So, then I got the binder down and I straightened that out. And one day I was in Wainwright, and we had an awful good guy. He had a brand new McCormick binder out there. The McCormick Deering it was. And people kept, it was quarter of a mile, it was in the edge of town. He kept all the machines out there. And people kept swiping off it, swiping off it, you know. I golly, he says, there's nothing left. I went out to see. He and I went out to see what he had left out there. He just had that plant for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what you wanted?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I didn't tell him that's just what I wanted. Well, I said, you got something left there. Yeah, he said, I got something left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, what do you want for that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now, you might have rung it right in the fall. Well, he said, if you want to give them for $20. I said, okay, I'll take it. So I gave them $20.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got the platform?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I put that on the line. It was a brand new binder. And, of course, it didn't do me much good, but I took it. And I had the best binder in the country, and the neighbor told me, he said, I don't give a darn what I have to pay for that binder. He said, I want that binder when you have that safe. And nobody would make ten cents on anything. Twenty -five blow and wind blow.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's the day we had our safe. And the ball of the year. And snow for Alberta, of course, it was only a stay, I suppose, maybe two or three days. Well, it was thirty -five blow. I know, but it had blow away. Or twenty -five, yeah. Twenty -five to that day.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, it was a desperate day. And they didn't feel like spending any money, you know, for a binder that time of the year. So, of course, I know he would have gave it if somebody would offer more. He put down fourteen dollars for it. He got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For how much?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: For the binder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For fourteen dollars?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. That's depression for you. That's why we suffered.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I had one cow. I had a bunch of cows and I sold them. The first calf was old. Nice looking heifers. Three year old. Coming fresh. And they sure ripped it too. Thirty -five dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: For the heifers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The heifers?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. Thirty -five dollars. We have had them sold for sixty and two. Yeah, and then, oh, I paid some for horses. That's how we got horses too. But this was when you were selling off. Selling off movie down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did that sale make you feel pretty bad? You didn't get any money or anything?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You get numb. You know what to expect. And I tell you, that thirty -five below, you were so glad to be escaping. It was just part of the loss. I knew she wanted to get out too. Me wanted to get out. I would mind. I'd stay here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I figured, I said, look, if you come up here, if you take our sheriff's estate, what would happen?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It would be all swallowed up to pay Honest John's debts. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was that mortgage on the place, and we owed the bank, and a few other places. Well, I paid straight in debt for the bank for it. I know you did, dear. But then we got out of debt this mortgage off our bank for the same percent interest. And we got down here with a thousand dollars. So we were better off. We had lost the whole thing. We stayed up there. We had been just swallowed up. And had that miserable weather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were you in favor of getting this place right away, or did you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I was. I was. Anything with a home.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: A place to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you find out that you were going to be able to get this place? Did you come down here for the will? Or, who was the executor? Was it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, listen, my dad had a place over on Central Ridge. Five hundred and sixty acres over there, you know. That had to be sold. Nice buildings on it and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see the buildings on it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I suppose you saw it there. Never.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And, of course, my brother could wreck everything as quickly as he could. But you see, we all knew we were going to get a sheriff's estate. And we took this place for our sheriff's estate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that out up there, or did you come down here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, a stepmother. They knew it pretty well before he went back up there. He was here a week or so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After the funeral?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, he came down for the funeral, you see, and talked it over with the brothers. And the stepmother told you then what she had. There was no will, and she was going to get, what was it, five thousand dollars in the car.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She won five thousand K, and then she had the adopted girl, little girl, you know, and dad. And I know they thought a lot of the little girl. I don't blame them. And, of course, they wanted a three thousand something for her. Well, that was her thing. And the car and the house. And then they got the brand new car, the Dead Head. And, oh, there's different things.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But she was very generous, and she said. Oh, she was the best in the whole outfit right there. Just wonderful. And she stuck up for me just like her mother. Clear and complete.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just like she was your brother?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Like her mother. Like my mother. Stuck up for me. She liked him because she never was around him by any time. Well, she said I was the biggest help she ever had.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Best boy in the family. Well, I know, and I knew that she'd never get along with Helmer. Even if you lived a hundred miles away, you'd come back and raise, you'd like a yellow jacket coming in, you know, and stinging everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't she like him? Did he give her her own time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, listen, a ton of what happened. Helmer married. He made the worst mistake he ever made in his life. He took Helmer and Ethel, just a little newborn girl, as far as being a little bride, you know, she didn't know too much about housekeeping. Moved in with him, with his dad, and this other stepmother. Well, you can just imagine. That wouldn't last. That didn't last. And Helmer is hard to get along. He's just grand as a brother -in -law up there. He's just real nice to us. He's done the nicest things. But, oh, that was a terrible mistake. And he thought, well, Ethel, I can understand her. That mother -in -law of his was so efficient. She was just a whiz. She could cook for twelve people like I could cook for one. And Ethel just stood around there and didn't know what to do. And then she accused Ethel of not helping her. Well, poor Ethel was just overwhelmed by her speed and not knowing what to do and feeling out of place. And she just went up to her room and sat in the room and read.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, and after dinners or so, you know, she'd grab a book or something and read that. And hear the dirty dishes and everything on that. God, my stepmother couldn't stand that. Believe me, that had to be cleaned off and cleaned and put away before you did anything else. But Ethel never could understand that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And of course, naturally, Helmer would stick up for her. Listen, Ethel learned that you had a good housekeeper and a good cook. But, boy, she was just newly married and just no experience. And her mother had died and left her as a young girl. She had a stepmother, too. But she said she never worked. She did help a little on a thrashing cruise. She knew how, but then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how had your father gotten this place? How did that happen?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, he bought it from Grandpa. He bought it from his father. Well, not exactly. Grandpa gave it to his son, Ed. And then Ed S. traded it. He traded it to... For land on Central Ridge. Well, he... Well, he sold it to Ed, to Dad, for I think $2 ,800 or something. And, you know, you couldn't get that much cash out of it in this country at that time. There was only 25 acres broke here when I come here. See, this here whole hill here, I broke up after I come here. Well, stay in the story there. And that five acres over there. Well, anyway, he got that...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He gave Ed the money then for this place. Yeah, he gave Ed the money.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then Ed could take that and move to the Central Ridge. That is, they could make a deal with that old bachelor over there to pay so many thousand for the place. I don't know what it was now, but maybe $3 ,500 or something like that. I think that's what it was. I think that's what the old bachelor gave for it. And he built a house on there and he sold it for the same price.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed did?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, the bachelor. Oh, yeah. And then Ed comes along and I think he got it for the same money. And he paid so much cash. See, whatever the guy wanted, it didn't matter if it was all cash. All Ed had to do was to go good for it. And his...Dad's credit on any of the banks around there was good. Good as cash, see. So he could take it into town, you know, and the place was Ed. And so, but I know he wouldn't do that for Helmer and I, but he did it for his brother. But then Ed didn't stay there. He decided to go... Well, he stayed there for eight years, I think it was. And he got $8 ,000. A fellow coming along and he said... Old tires, I guess. He got...He paid that for eight years. He got $8 ,000 off for it. And then he made the rations for Dad to take care of the old folks.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that? They went to live with your father or what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, yeah, I think that's what it was. And Dad had it rigged up some way. He built onto this house over here on Central Ridge Sea. And he took them in there and...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Ed originally was supposed to support the old people for the rest of their life for having this farm. But then... But then he sold the farm. Yeah, but then he sold it. Then he sold the farm to his Dad. And then his Dad eventually ended up by taking care of the old people. He even took them clear out when he went practicing as a doctor to...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He took them to the coast.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And the grandmother died out there. And the old man, though, he finally ended up with Ed after his Dad died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how...Your Dad, was he one of the oldest of the brothers?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the oldest?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He was the oldest. And Ed was the oldest. Yeah, Ed was the youngest. There were six brothers in the family. Yeah. And so anyway... But Ed went with the $8 ,000. It didn't last too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Up in Alberta?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, he finally went up there. But he said...He told me he gave his sister $2 ,000, the night of them, to... And he shipped some cattle down there. To help him out. To help her out. She had the other brother who had cured of this too. Now I had to have the idea and she said they paid him a percent for those cattle. And she tells the truth. Well, maybe your husband might have said that. But he still had a lot of money coming back out of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But so Ed didn't...was not the executor of the will then? Who was the executor of your father's will?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Helmer. Helmer was. Yeah. I see. Yeah, well I consented to that. You did not... I'd rather he'd have the whole darn thing than have Ed have it. Yeah, he did. Because of the deal that he pulled off on the binder bed and see. You know, he said he'd give it to me after... He got his part of the crop off. He was through farming then, you see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But you... He sold the place. You two were of one mind about coming down here. You both agreed completely. There wasn't any... Well listen, I was halfway anywhere before this happened. Let's put it that way. I was about halfway on my journey in any direction before this happened. Steiner thought, well we might take that money and leave up here. But he didn't push it. He thought it was a pretty good idea to get down here, really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of strain on your marriage at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, we were pretty good. You knew you'd be staying together, whatever it was. Oh yeah, it's never any problem. You know, when you get to... In Canada, you just never think about getting divorced. It's one of those stable things. Yeah, remember my mother, things falling down or falling apart or what, you just carry on. Then the kinks come out as you keep on going. No, there was no strain on our marriage then. It was pretty good. First few years were... I don't know. First few years, it sounds like, were rougher. Yeah, with the two little kids so close together. I think they were harder. But then you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you were immature at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, I felt I was very mature.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For having those two kids and all that at that time?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. I mean, I was growing up at 12. I really was. I was suddenly, you know, with my little sister and being shifted around and being responsible. I really think I was older than most children from my age. No, I never felt immature. I felt inexperienced in some things, but that isn't the same as being immature.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, one thing is warmer down here. I knew it was warmer down here. And of course, we had mud and rain and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like the idea of coming back to the old home place here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Boy, I liked it, sure.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That seemed like a good... Yeah, nice place to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were coming home in a way, right?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I hadn't been back here for 20 years, though. Not even for a visit. And so... But I could stay there. Sure, I could stay there. But then I could stand there with the winners. Do everything. But toward last, I began to notice the cold more than I did. Before that.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I wonder why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you getting older?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I was 45. Was it... Were the... Did the other...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your neighbors, your friends up there, did they leave too or did they stick out?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, some of them stuck it out, but some left. And these Ukrainians all left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They left?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You betcha they did. And they just prospered like green bay trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After they left?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they suffered like everybody else did in the depression here, didn't they?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think they always had better gardens than the rest of us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they left?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But they left, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd they go?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Vancouver. Well, it's close to... You know, newest ministers on the way up to Vancouver. They got a mushroom farm. George was an excellent farmer. He knew, he learned how to raise mushrooms, and they just made money hand over fist. And he had... He had three girls. Yes, there was two girls and two boys at home then, or at least there was about three anyhow with them. And they usually had some cousins or something to pick these mushrooms every day. Maybe they had a neighbor or two. They had to be picked, you know. And oh, they prospered. He had one barn and one shed, and then he had two. It's a whole story. It's just fascinating, those mushrooms and how he grow them. And we learned a lot, of course, not that we ever did. But he did well. This was George... The Dora. The one you like, yeah. The funny one, and the one that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a pretty close friend of yours, eh?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. Oh, yeah. He was always sociable. He loved people. He was one of these kind like me. He just loved the desert and the gold people. He's going to town, he'd go by. Come on, Stine, go to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were he unusual for the Ukrainians? I mean, in his being open to other people? Or were most of them open?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, they vary. Now, there's that other one that lived close to us. He was a regular... Oh, I don't know. He was just like a clam, and his wife was worse. But George, I think, is more open than a lot. He was a matchmaker. Now, that's something. You didn't have a matchmaker here in Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see how I miss those things? What was he doing? Getting people married?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's right. Ukrainians would come over from Ukraine, you know, or had them in here too long. Couldn't get a girlfriend. George knew everybody. He knew them up north. He knew them in Edmonton. The Ukrainians. He knew all the Ukrainians. He was a wheel amongst the Ukrainians. Traveled all the time. Kept up with his friends. And they always come. They come anywhere close. They went there. There was never a wheel. I don't think they didn't have company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, George, he was always trying to get this if you had a daughter that wasn't moving too fast, you know?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: George would know some strong person up there. And, well, you know, there's so -and -so's son over there. Maybe you ought to meet him. And he'd make pretty good matches. One fell apart, but then he said, Gosh, you know, he shouldn't have beat that girl. He said, I didn't think he'd do that. He got mad at her, and he waltzed her a few times. And she wouldn't take it. She was about second generation. But he did. But you talked about the Ukrainians as being, a lot of them being first generation and pretty backward in their ties to the old country. You're talking about like that guy that made his mother sleep on a floor while he slept on a couch. Yeah, that's her. That's his wife's sister. I know. So, but George was a real progressive fellow then. But he said he couldn't stand living around those people. That's why he moved down in our district where there wasn't another Ukrainian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that true?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They weren't living around Plaxto?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There wasn't a - Yes. They come there to Plaxto. They come there to Plaxto, but there wasn't another Ukrainian around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far away were the rest of them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: About 60 miles up there. Oh, that far. I see. There was a few in town on the railroad section, a few Ukrainians. But he just moved and just made a clean sweep. He said he was going to get in and just get clear away from them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Because he didn't like - He thought that too much drinking and oh, and they weren't progressive enough. And they didn't, he was interested in politics and all they wanted to do was party and they weren't all like that. But he thought it was good to get his children. He was ambitious for his children. He had very smart little children. Yeah, you know, I was going to say about his boy and I thought an awful lot of his boys. And he went to school. I think he told them that. Yeah, I told them. Yeah, where I helped him. About that kid, remember I told you that teacher had a pet and this kid didn't get enough help with his fractions and - Repletic. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He just couldn't understand fraction business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the method, is it from George Godores that you learned how to do the walls?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh sure, everything.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He and his wife, his wife was really smart in a lot of ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You had to listen to her too.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She was so particular, their walls were beautiful. And she always mixed the mud.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she teach you how to do that? What was the directions that she gave you for doing it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, for one thing, after she talked to me up there, I had an old log house there that would blow between the logs and things. And I said, you know, if I use some of that mud to talk about, maybe I could use that thing to put grain in things. So I mudded it up inside. She and George came along one morning. I think every other morning or so, they'd either come or go, you know. Go to town past that. And so I said, hey, come on in here and have a look.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what do you think of the mud I put on?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was dry dense. Miss Godores took a very good close look, you know. And after she got through shit, it looks all right. You know, it mustn't crack, see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And you get too much mud and not enough straw, you know. The main thing is to take your clay and put all the straw you can possibly hold in it. And clean straw, you know. Not a mixture of stuff in the straw. Nice clean straw. Well, not only that, but you don't... It's mud, you know. You put water in it. And you don't dump it. Just go through. Tell me how the directions, the way you do that, the way you make the walls. I never heard that. You told my friend, but not me when I was here before. Yeah, well, there's an awful lot to it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well... You mix your clay first. There's lots of clay in here. Just like you'd mix anything else. Like you're making a mud pile. You get your mud mixed up nice till it's thick enough. And it's just like a gravy, see. Then you start putting straw in. And not too much is what's done. Not a big lump. Be sure that every straw and stuff gets in there. Well, now the way they had, they had to dug out and made a big vat like, you see. Like a pit, I would say. And they take some of the straw in their armor, you know. Two of them. I want to be walking this way and the other one this way. Barefooted. The women. Barefooted. Dressed nice. And dress it up about a foot, you know. And just walk barefooted in that mud. It'd be about eight inches deep, maybe. And catch all the straw and pull it down. I suppose they'd kind of grab it with their toes like this, you know. And walk around until it got so that it was so full of straw that they'd hold them up. It was full. Okay, there you are, men. There's your pit. Go ahead and put it on the walls. So that's what they'd go out there and load up. Their arms were this here mud and go out and slap it on. And George told me, he said, you got a good arm, you'll be alright. But he said, you got to have a good arm. You got to throw that thing right in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Throw it on the wall?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Good and hard. He had laughs on this house. I put laughs on. They had little poles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he said in the old country, years and years ago, he said, they used to put little pegs like that in the edge of the log, see?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Every edge of the log and put it on that way. But he said, but I thought, well heck, I'll put a laugh on it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: We were figuring it out the other day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two dollars and, what was it, two dollars and eighty -five cents for a whole plant? For a laugh?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, there's only fifty cents a bunch and you know you get about this much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Laughs. Yeah. And they're four feet long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was to do the whole room there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And you get the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did you do this room too?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I did the whole house. I didn't know that. I didn't know that. Well, yeah, a lot of guys tell me, no, this is the best room. I looked in here before, but I never... Did you see the... Yeah, but I didn't notice it in here. Oh, yes.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Everything is...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I told her I would put a board on that. Oh, she didn't. She didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see? How big, how big, what consistency is it when you actually put it on the wall? What's the consistency?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, like I told you.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You fix it by the first, then you put the straw. Well, that's all there is to it. Then you put it on the wall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you put it on like is it, will it just come out from pieces of mud or is it almost like sheets?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, you take, you just put it on the wall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You scoop it, you put it on the board, didn't you, Snyder?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Kind of like a, like a scallop. And then you scoop it up and throw it. Yeah, throw it. There's something that will pick up, just like a piece of... You can see my glass picking up here. George told me that it shouldn't really be like this, you know. Well, yeah, but I said, uh, hey, I got to have a screw gun down because of the log. It's not to pick up the spot. Here, I want to show you. Now, there's no water dripping out. Here, I want to show you something. Now, on the other side, that log wall is pretty straight. But on this side, you put a straight edge from the top down, and it'd be about four inches, like in just the wall. Or maybe five inches. But you have to build out something to hold the log.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you have to gradually come in, see?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And then you have to fill that space in with mud. That's where you have to have a good arm, going right through that. That's why I put these four inches apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year did you do that in? Well, about, uh, 36, didn't we, Snyder?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So shortly after you came down. Yeah, well, look, practically the first year. We came out here in 35. And then the next winter, we started on the bedroom. She says, that winter, she says, you know, you've got to mud this house. And I said, okay. So I thought then I got all the directions from George. He couldn't come down. He was coming down to do it. But when he does it, he won't leave it rough like that. He takes it, when he gets all the mud on the walls, up through the ceiling there, he takes a square board, you know, with square head. But it's dry. And, well, it's got to dry first. We had it in July. It took about a month to dry. It takes a long time to dry. It's thick. This mud isn't a quarter of an inch like this Latin plaster here. It's thick. Some places might be an inch and a half to two inches or more. Oh, yeah. It's irregular because of the logs. And it's got to be perfectly dry. Well, all right then. When it's perfectly dry, you have to sprinkle water on it. Until you get the outside wet. When that outside gets wet, you take a square board and you square that off. Like this. Until you get a square. And I could see where George had had that. So it just looked like a window plate. Just as smooth as a window plate. I tried to tell her, I'll do that. Oh, no. She said, you leave it. I like it rough. Well, it's nice rough. I like it. And she wanted me to leave it. I said, okay, I'll leave it just the way it is. So I get her. Yeah. But if George did it, you'd swear it was nothing but. There were other things too that the New Granians did that you found. And here's another thing I want to tell you that I told the other guy. Was this got full of water here one day, see. The roof on top there. The water was coming down to me and everything. And there was a shake out of here. It was running on top of this roof. We didn't have a light or anything there. No, I think I put my finger up just about where the light is, see. And the whole thing was hanging down like it was hanging. Well, it was a bottle of shtiter. I never noticed it hanging at all. It was too deep. So I just said, well, all right. I brought a tub in. I said, I brought a tub in, sat it down there, and put my finger up. And here it come down just like you turned the faucet on. I stuck the tub right underneath and got one to quit running. Well, I just wiped it back, you know, filled it up like that. Pushed it up again. Never touched it since. So it's just the way it was. Yeah, that's great. And you know any other plaster that would have been on the floor.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, dear, tell them about finishing there. Let me tell them. Yeah, I'll let you.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I have to put a finishing coat on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it's lime, isn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Lime and sand. Oh, yeah. And horse manure. Yeah, well. And a little bit of clay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't there a little bit of clay?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, well. About a fourth of each.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, a little bit. No, no, listen. I'll tell you what George said. This is a finishing coat on top of the. On top of the. Now when you get it all, you shouldn't have a spoon like George had. But I never, I left it rough. That takes more paint, more of everything, you know. Well, now you want something to hold the paint. I didn't know it that time, but I know now, see. So you mix up, this is the third coat, you might say. I don't know, second coat. Second coat. Well, you take a pail, you put a third horse manure in this pail.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Or is on grass, no green.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And, well, of course, George in particular, you don't need to be that particular. I wouldn't. And you don't have to. But, yeah, they said another thing. He said to be real good, he said get a three year old. Because they chew their food better, see. Well, you don't need it that way, but it doesn't matter what the horse eats. If it eats grass, that's what you want. You want that grass cut up, see, and it's all juicy and everything. And you put a third of each of these, a third of sand, third of clay, and a third of this horse manure. And you mix that up, see. Mix that all up, like you would mix, like if you was cooking something. Like you're mixing a batter for this. Batter for anything. And that's got to be a certain thickness. Well, they put it on with their hands. But you know the thickness, you could put it on with a brush if you wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that provides a primer coat for the paint?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. That's what it does. And so, some guys think you put that right in with the coat on. The first coat that you put on. Well, then you've lost it. You haven't done anything right. You've lost the whole thing. And all you need, now George says, just keep the horse in overnight. And they would catch that manure if they had that thing. That's about all you need for a whole place. But for this big house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then the lime would go on top of that? Hey? Then you put white lime on top of that? That would be your paint for that, right?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Remember now, this is green, more or less, once put on. It's got to dry perfectly dry. And then when you start painting, you put lime on. Well, you can put it on with a brush. But I found that started out with a spray. And all that would have been the thing. But I didn't have anything to strain the lime. You know, to get all the things you could plug it up with or anything. But if I had that, I could have gone over it and just sprayed it. It's got quite a smell, the old thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It just sticks on right now. And after that, you can put any kind of paint on it. And the Ukrainians, they never put anything else but lime on it. Lime is their paint. Yes, and they've done... They never painted it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It is white wash. It is white wash. Sometimes they'd mix it with skim milk. And sometimes they'd put a little bluey. You know, this old -fashioned bluey, used to blue clothes, they'd put that in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what could make it pale blue? Well, were there any other old country Ukrainian ways that George knew that you learned? Or that you saw and admired?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Building fences.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember that fence, that hog fence we put around the garden?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, but now listen, I was going to finish this first. Oh, I'm sorry. George told me when I told him, you know, they never did the ceiling. I'm the first one to do the ceiling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, George, why don't you put the ceiling in?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They had come and growed ceilings. We never do. We put just, no, well, it was what they call regular ceiling stuff. We had it here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: B -joint. See, and this house had B -joint in it. See, it's special for ceiling. And he said, we painted blue. Well, I want mud. So I went along to see that every board was solid. He said, be sure, he said, every board is solid. And I went along and nailed up anything that was loose, and then I nailed it solid. But I put a lathe on about every three inches. And George said they never did put any ceiling in. So that's the Canadians, the way they do their house. And they've done that all their life up there. They never put a ceiling in. Well, Mrs. Godora, she gave me the idea, though. Well, she got to thinking. I said, that's funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You never put a ceiling in?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And she said, well, yes. She said there was one guy who put a ceiling in. And he put little round poles in the ceiling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know the idea of little round poles?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You put them in, I hold them peeled. Put them in to hold the mud. And so I said, well, that's the idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not use lathe?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So I said, well, boy, I'll put lathe on. So I put lathe on, and I've never had no trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to say, remember that hog fence we made?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It was hog proof. We took it around the garden. We put down poles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know how tough their tree is?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, we just simply cut them leaves and everything. And we just shoved them out the bunions down the pole. And then I guess they overlapped. And then they dried, and they just made a stiff barrier so that the hogs wouldn't go through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that the way you do it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Tell you, my name was tight as you can. Yeah, just as tight as you can jam them in between. And up as high as you want. And up as high as you want. That was one. I put in four and a half acres. You know, hogs, they're so hard to fence for. And when they dry, they get so stiff, and those little branches.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Those poles, they grew not big around, and very tall, slim. Maybe a lot of little branches on, but just cut them off at the bottom. Throw them on, you'd have them long enough they'd stick way behind the wagon. Load them up, and I remember that's how Laura got the wagon backed up on her. Here, you know. I told Laura, of course, don't play it's warm day. And I'd cut a lot and put on there, and had the horses hooked up yet in there. I guess I should have had them on the hook. But I was so busy, didn't have time for nothing. That was warm up the hill system. I could cut an awful lot of trees if nobody bothered me. And I thought the kids would listen to me. And I says, don't play under there. And of course, that was an ideal little house since you're there. And they were having fun, and I was maybe fifty yards away from them. Cutting and dragging them up, and cutting and dragging them up, you know. I'd get a big load on that. And I had a pretty good load on that. And I looked and saw this one horse, I just never did like her anyway. And if she ever got her bridle or anything hooked up around the time, on anything, she had to back up that's almost, you can back and never quit. Just keep backing, see. And there was those kids playing behind. And I saw that and I began to run. Run for the horses, you know. I said, they should start backing. She was backed like a bullet. Well, the line got underneath the tongue, see, and she started pulling back. Gosh, I got there and she was a back. And here she backed up on Laura's chest. Yes, she just went to crown. And she was only about thirty -six pounds. Yeah, on that big load, you know, right on the heavy hind wheels, narrow wagon, narrow wheel. About five years old. And, you know, but of course she knew I was mad because I told her not to get under there. And when she heard me coming and all that and got out of there, I kicked that horse in the stomach and I pulled her around and got her out of there. And boy, she got up and she ran around with her mouth open. Just about as far as meeting the door and she got her breath. She hadn't been scared to death of me being mad at them being underneath there. She wouldn't have done that. But boy, she knew that she was in the wrong. She got up in front and got her breath. Boy, that was a relief. When I had to kill that horse, I could have... She wasn't broken. I was so mad at that horse, I had an awful good horse. But this made it dirty. If she didn't run, she wouldn't have gotten her breath back for ages. Well, the way she got up, you see, and started stirring, I, oh, that was the best thing in the world. She got up by force. She just made herself get up right now, see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: These Ukrainians would build remarkable things with this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, those other Ukrainians that moved in, Pete, remember the bugs there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They made buildings, sheds and buildings with...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: With that brush.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But they weren't quite as brushy and then they murdered them. They just put the poles up and they trimmed it back somewhat, but still it wasn't sticking out. They left the left branches to get... They left branches to retain the mud, and they mudded on both sides. And that's what they built. It was just like a wall.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: For a chicken house, you can think of like that. Yeah, and the floor in the grain room, mind you, was hard as the floor here. They cracked it in. Oh, boy.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I'm going to have to go real soon because Laura's going to start worrying about me. I said I'd be home at 5, 5 .30 in the morning. But I want to ask you just a little bit about what happened after you came down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you took this place, did things start turning around for you financially, or how long was it before things started looking up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I don't think... Well, I'd say it was about three years we got more cattle, sold a little more grain. Until they got rich. Well, yeah. It wasn't real good. He got more land broke off. But we never were in the buying, such a buying for food. Never like that. It was gradually improving.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, the first thing I knew, 25 acres, I thought they had more than that. And I said, my gosh, Dad had cleared this. That is, he had cut the brush off over there. Stuffed it as high as his table. All over. And I was looking at it, and the horse was coming running through there, my gosh. I says, they run into one of those stumps and get gutted, you know. And I says, I have to knock them out. I hit them with a sledge hammer, I could knock the little ones out. Gee, Wynton, Forrest was just a little...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he, eight years old?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He was eight years old, but they... Oh, he liked it. Be out with me.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: But we got on our feet. We really, we never got in depth down here. We kept out of depth. We never got in depth. And we, you got the little bit of money, once in a while, you work a little bit. You traded with, you traded, we did a lot of trading here of you and Corey with work. And your neighbors would haul things for us. And we, we just kind of moved along. Well, and Corey. That was the war was on. That was when my choice was ready for high school. When Corey. And that's when we moved down. Corey. We never made a fortune in here. And it's a good thing we got out from here. Because the people started, it wasn't long after that, this big ranch sold. And the people that moved down, sold their land, they gradually acquired 2 ,000 acres down here. In here. And it's just lucky we got out from here. In other words, the community was on its last legs. It was on its last legs just about the time we left. The people moved out of here right from last. They did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they move? During the war?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, they did war work. His cousin moved, Dixon's moved. And the sons, the young sons, a lot of the young people left. They left. There was three of us left right away. And then there was a few more left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get, did you, were you able to make good money in Portland?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yeah. Oh yeah, I made good money. You went for work right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, what did, what did you, who did you work for?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, I worked for a different outfit. He worked in a shipyard. But here. Then he worked as a carpenter, worked, and then he worked as a reveled metal. That's about the size of a car. And when I worked on an outfit, why, I put my eight hours there.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And when I got home, I worked maybe another eight hours. Oh, not quite eight hours. Well, I worked all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked on the house?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He's working on them. On different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you own a house there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, we bought a house within a year. Yes. And we sold it within five years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part of Portland? You know where Mount film is? We had a maple wood on the west side, ground here, or Beaverton is?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yeah. Well, we were about five miles from Beaverton. That's where we bought our first house. Well, I'll tell you how it is. And then we built one, bought one in Stark Street. Didn't live in it. Then we went to Park Road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where that is?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Sure. We had a house there for about five years. Then we moved on this side of Rocky Butte, but still own a very small place there in a beautiful grapefruit block. That's on 100, that's about 90, between 90 seconds and 80 seconds. Just off, just off the whole thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you, and did you, were you able to go to work too soon after you went there?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

speaker: Sam Schrager

He came back here and of course, I was, I'm 15 years between us and I knew that while he was here, you know, Social Security wasn't much then and he had invested everything, you know, every nickel. speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

words: Yeah. Oh, I thought, no, I didn't like it at all. I thought it wasted life, but oh well.

But you've got to feed the creatures. Mrs.

It's a terrible waste of human potential. 12, 14 years.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, you know, when I came up here from Port Knott, I had to, in mind, the first thing was to get running water. I like to make money, I'm a grub. Hot and cold water. I'm sorry, I like to make money, and there's always work for me and good money. I get hot and cold water, running water in here, I've got it just the same as you have in the city.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, yeah, you can mix up with a lot of different people. There's lovely people. I'll tell you one thing about cities. I've had it happen in Portland. speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Dear, they never bother me at all. I have my own little things I look after.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And pretty soon, you get well acquainted, they expect it, see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I don't know about your friends, but mine never bother me. They're usually trying to keep me going, keep me floating. Think of all the nice people.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I know, thinking at my age, the idea of Lauren being separated for half a year would really kill me. Yes, it would be terrible if you were younger, but look, it's another case of finances. He come out here, he had about $100, $98 Social Security, every nickel we own. Just wiped out, been putting in the bank for years. He wiped it all out, put it in machinery. That's all he had. To get this farm going, and to put the water in, he went in debt and, fine, it's $44 a month, I have like $66. He couldn't live on it. I couldn't possibly exist on it. And he was having a struggle. I just had to work. There was no choice. I had plenty of friends. I had two daughters there at that time. You just, well, that's it. If it's the only place he can be happy, go. I have my friends and I have my children. My work I like, so you just keep on going. And he loves pine trees and he loves ducks and I don't think he's worrying about me straight away and I'm not worrying about him, so just go on. As younger people know, it would be terrible and be a crazy thing. But then I'm used to, as I say, there's a background in our family of men going to see and my husband, father like that. We're kind of used to them, you know, a little bit touched in the head. My dad, he was so restless, he could just hardly wait to go tramping around. I've had a lot of experience about him sometimes too, I can tell you now. Very, very interesting.

words: In the 30's, when you came back here and lived here, what did the community life consist of in Park? For you, what was it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, there was a few dances. I don't know. These guys here all had four left feet, I think. They weren't much. The dances weren't much fun. And there were such clods. These old... I tell you, as I say, that time I was out here chopping wood. Steiner was late coming home. Didn't have quite enough to last me. And this guy calmly sat there and watched me chop wood. You didn't tell me that. No. That would never happen in Canada.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just sat there and watched him do it and talk to you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, yeah. He talked to me. One of these clods, lumberjack clods, for yellowberry. Two dads, the one whose wife divorced. But Steiner had worked out. That was the first summer I was here.
