I was perfectly capable of doing it. But as a principal, it just would never have happened in Canada. And the difference too that you made to it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Another thing, I never went to a place. If I saw a woman chopping wood, I would go right over and say, you better let me chop wood. And I remember getting a job, I think it was in Alberta, yeah. And there was a fellow from the United States, from Southern Idaho, or some place down in there. And he had a wife, and she was used to having things down there. And she was going to have a baby for very long. And I hired out to him through the employment office in Calgary. And I got out there, and he went into Calgary, and I missed him, see. So I got out there to the ranch, and here she was carrying a big slop pail, out about as far from here to the gate out here, you know. That electric gate to feed the pigs. Then she'd have to lift it up over a place about this high and pour it into a truck. And I started coming to this, I was just walking up, you know. And I said, wait a minute here, you better set that down, I'll take it over. And so, oh she said, I can do it now. I said, you better leave that. And so anyway, I think I talked her out of it, and I took it and emptied it. And oh, she thought that was wonderful, helping her. Well, my gosh, she didn't go over a month or so, because she had a baby. And that fellow, that guy coming there into Calgary had nothing to go in there for. He ordered so many men and everything, and here I was out there before he was. And so...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, Snyder always was beautifully polite, and Forrest, I told my kids, I said, both boys and, the boy and the girl, never go to a place that you don't share the household work. And get in and help the woman, you know, and if you're a girl. And Forrest helped, told him, I said, you go over there and this, you say, they're going to be milking cows, you help milk cows. These clogs around here, I had to educate these kids. I'd just tell them, melon goodness and that bunch, I said, get with it now, and you can get in and help. And they can learn to rustle around and do a few things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the custom in Canada?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why in your communities to do that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes. They were very good. The men were just beautifully trained. As I say, I would call a man by his first name, and he would call me Mrs. Sawn, and then Jack Peach would come. If I needed water, if I needed wood, if I needed a child looked after, I mean, fell down, got mud on it or something, pick it up, bring it in. Just do everything. And these clogs here would just sit there and look at you. I thought they'd be like him because that was just excellent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that just around here? Was it the same way around Central Ridge? I mean, were the American boys pretty?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, Central Ridge was different.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: This here was just a bunch of ignoramuses. Grew up, they figured, oh, that's a woman's job. Oh, that's a woman's job.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's a woman's job. Let her do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that the old Scandinavian kind of way of doing it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, I think so. This family down here is third or fourth generation German, and boy, they're just a kiddie like that. They had five boys and one girl, and boy, they're marvelous workers. They're German, but boy, those boys don't do it. Well, Germans are, as a whole, a good man. They're marvelous men. You don't need to worry about that. Still, it's just endless. They're up bringing. But you also talked before about how you couldn't be friends with the men around here either because of the suspiciousness. A woman has to be much more discreet. You couldn't show too much friendliness or laugh or joke. Just the most innocent little remark. She would never dare say anything out of line. Not that I would up there, but I don't know what kind of women they associated with when they were youth. I wonder if that was another old country way. It could be. A woman had to be just very, very circumspect and old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the local women here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: The other women were just as quiet and just as discreet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you develop the same kind of close friendships here that you put up in Vainwright? Or was there a difference?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was a difference. There was one older woman and I were very close. Mrs. Strath, one of my best friends now, it's taken me, it took me 20 years before I got very close to her. This woman that came to the newspaper today, it was on jury day. We're very close now. I see her every day. But when I first came here, she was completely absorbed in her mother, father, and her kids. And friends. She never broke through to her. And she's one of the most intelligent women in here, but she was just so close. I never got to know her. It wasn't. And there was a, I don't know, I wouldn't say I was close to her, but the thing was, his cousin Mary, a divorced woman, those two little kids, and she came here just the same week we came down from Canada. We were thrown together in desperation because we were the two newcomers and the last ones in. And we were quite friendly but not close. But we were, she'd call me out. And there was other friends here that I'd see quite often. And I was, they're neighbors, good neighbors, but not close friends. Ethel, Helmer's wife, was one of my close friends. She was one of my best ones. She was really nice. I was closer to her than anybody else here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But Helmer wasn't here at that time, was he? Didn't he just go into Moscow around then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well Moscow was, but I mean that's what I mean. I'd go out there maybe every week or two and I'd see Ethel. Sometimes all friends and I, sometimes we'd be out there every week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well do you think that the adversity up in Wainwright and the desperation of the situation brought people closer together?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, it did. It did, but still they're prairie open. I'd been in three different, at least three different sections I've lived in for a year in Alberta. They're all friendly. One friend I had, I still have one of my oldest friends, is where I taught last in Alberta. She come out here this summer all the way from Seattle. She saw her sister come out here, drove out to see me. And her sister died when I was to the funeral. No, they were everywhere I've been on the prairie. I've had close friends and I've had, I don't know, helpfulness. These people were kind in a way. They were kind, but I, Mrs. Wist and I were about the friendliest in here. Ethel was a friend of mine, but I was very close to her. What about that Torgeson family, which from what others like, other people have told me were one of the real, typical, old country...

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think it was them.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They were gone by the time he got here. They were gone.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I never knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, Torgeson you say?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, Torgeson, yeah. Well, I... The man knows them. I don't. I met, I talked to Torgeson years ago. And he knew I'd been down Southern Idaho. That was about 1915. I came back from Southern Idaho. And I thought I'd come in and see Grandpa and Grandma, you know. And I stopped in there to see them. And, well, he thought I'd have a nice talk. So he asked me about growing alfalfa. And I thought there's nothing to it, you know. I told him how they did down there. I said, new ground. They, uh, sow, then they sow soil with it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You know, they have a bunch of soil. Oh, that's, why they sow the soil. You sow the, you know that alfalfa has to be inoculated with nitrogen. And there has to be some of those little buns in it. So that's why they picked some of the soil where alfalfa was growing. So it passed along. But now you can get it in a package and scatter it in. That's the idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when he went to go to Torgeson and say to you, was it at the time he gave you a pitchfork and says, or a hammer and told you to help?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's telling you?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, they hadn't grown any hair then.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, but it wasn't at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went down to visit and you always got somebody to help them?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, he never asked me to help him. Well, somebody was complaining about him the other day and they said he always, whoever went there to visit, he always got them to help him. I must have been strapped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he, but were most of the people in here at that time still Norwegian when you came back in the 30s?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So in other words, it didn't have the friendliness that you had experienced elsewhere. It was more ingrown. I tell you, I think, I think that in their quiet way, they did have a concern for you. Now, like my old Scotch grandmother, she wasn't very, she wasn't a cuddly kind of a grandmother, but she'd always see that you were fed, that you had a good supper. She'd always see there was plenty of quilts on the bed. Maybe if she'd go up there and she wasn't quite, she'd feel your feet. And if she wasn't quite satisfied, she'd bring up a nice iron wrapped up in an old salt to keep your feet warm. You know, I think they had it here, that quiet kind of kindness. They were pretty good about seeing that you got fed. Helmer, she's good old instant food for Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The minute you get there, you got coffee and when did you eat last?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's the principle. There is a kindness there that I appreciate, but it doesn't catch on fire exactly right away. It doesn't ignite.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, Helmer wasn't treated the best when he was here either. You know, there's some people take advantage of a guy that's making it by hard work.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He worked awful hard all the time. Helmer will go out of the way to help a neighbor too. Oh yeah. He did that way. And he and Mrs.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Rich, who's talking about it, they always got along fine. And Helmer had nice horses and he farmed this place on the air. And he did a nice job. He put it all to beans. They only had twelve acres out here. And he put it all to beans. And I know a doll lived down here. And I think it was a doll that said, you know, he says, that's the straightest, nicest road. He said you can see them for long distance. He says I haven't seen anything like that since I left some eastern country. Well, I says over on Central Ridge. That's the way they used to do. We used to always do. We had lots of land. But if we put forty acres into beans or corn or anything like that, it was all done nice, straight roads, everything. And Helmer had horses that would go up that hill there, just as good as some horses go on the flat ground. And he thought nothing of it. He just didn't have any. Boy, they were good horses. But they took good care of them, fed them good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that Helmer had the same passion for work that you did? The same real bizarre kind of work?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He worked harder.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And he figured that if he didn't get up in the morning, you know, around three o 'clock in the morning if he had to, to get things done, why, he'd start to death. Or his horses. Or his cows. He always feeds his horses and cows. Everything had to be fed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you worked harder than you did?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, there's nobody working. Well, he did. I worked hard, but maybe I didn't. Zada works hard, but Helmer makes himself work. You know, I mean, he breaks something up with his clumsiness or his roughness, and then he'll have to stop and fix it. That's what's the problem with Helmer. Yeah, I think he... He just goes in like a bull, whatever he does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that you enjoyed, that maybe Steiner enjoyed work a little more?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I think so, because he did. I enjoyed it. Yes, I think Helmer has to work. He likes to work. But, boy, sometimes it's kind of hard the way he does it. Whenever he's doing something, we go up there, it's broken.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: It can be brand new and it's broken. He's got to fix it up. He would cut with an eight -foot binder all day and then shock it in the evening.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Things like that. I mean, they're both very physically strong. But I was going to mention something about these people. Do you realize when I was in here, a lot of these people had never been any place but par. His cousin, except into a lumber camp. I don't know how much cultural uplift that is. His cousin lived there. He lived there. Then his dad. All their lives lived here. Dolls. All their lives there. And those jellibird, those clods that watched me chop wood. Born in here. Died in here. That sort of thing. His cousin over here. Born in here. And Tilda. And Mary did too. A guy that was raised in here. Her cousin down here. Born and raised here. And then their kids. Never been any place else. Never knew anything else but this. Mrs. Rist had to have two husbands and thirteen kids or twelve kids. I mean, she had a little uplift. She'd been up to Canada in a covered wagon. It must have made a big difference in the continent in their outlook on the land.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, that's just, yes. They were just, yeah, fools.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I mean, they were not malicious or anything like that. But it did something to their lives. Now that's why I say Mrs. Rist really had a spark in life. She and her first husband went up to Canada in a covered wagon. Oh, yeah, that's something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find you had more in common with her than the other women?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes, I did. Yet she was old enough to be with my mother. She was much more in common. She was a very interesting person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would talk about what? You would talk with her about?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Anything. She was well read. She'd read everything and been places and seen things. And very good outlook on life. And her girls were about my age. Yes, just the matter was she was just real good about it. She could talk about anything and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with most of the women, what did they talk about here then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Your children and recipes. The schoolhouse. How your kids were getting along.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's it.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's the whole thing. There was nothing else they ever talked about. And maybe that Goldie used to howl about her father -in -law. She didn't like him. And I'd have to console her. This little woman that was married to his cousin, he took her home into the house where his dad lived. It was his dad's farm. And I couldn't expect the old man to leave, but the kids got on his nerves. And she'd go over here and weep on my shoulder about it. I mean, there'd be little troubles like that. And I don't know. I didn't visit too much. Well, I'd see them. I suppose I'd see the women. I never went to Dalt once or twice. But the daughter used to come over here occasionally. You know, there was enough women. If you even saw them once a month, why that was it. That was enough. Yeah, that was enough. And everybody was very guarded and cautious. And they'd never mention a neighbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really? You don't think that there was gossip?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, they never did to me. And I don't think they did too much between themselves. There had been a few. Oh, there was another woman that left. There was a Mrs. Austin. Not Mrs. Austin. What was Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Austin's daughter's name?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Clara. Clara Williams. I was quite friendly with them. She was a school teacher. And Faye. Well, I wasn't so friendly with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she teach school here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, she taught after she left here. I was usually quite friendly with the school teachers here. I boarded one of them here, one or two of them here for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, um, what'd you leave out?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: She was a nice guy. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There was Art and John Williams. Now, they were quite friendly with us. But they were awfully poor. You know, Art still comes up, but he hadn't been up this year. Well, he's retired. That's why. He said he was going to retire. But they're people that we were quite friendly with. And these Dixons that went out to port and left here at the same time, we did. They both died now, but they weren't friendly with us. They were from Oklahoma, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They hadn't been here for that long?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, they were outsiders. But that's the one that really had the most life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that, so you found that you were friendlier in many cases with the newcomers than with the old timers?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yes.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And the conversation was livelier. And Cory, now he was from Oklahoma. Cory worked awfully good with him. Oh, he and I. His wife was just a pure character. She was the kind when I was getting the kids off to school, I was just going to relax and unwind. She called me up about 8 .30 in the morning. Well, I got the wash down till 4 a .m. And when you saw that washy, I don't know, she dipped it in and dipped it out and shook it a little, hung on. But she got it all done. Five kids. She was the cheeriest. She was a character. Kind -hearted.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Soup.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I remember one night where a car broke down. We went there at 2 o 'clock in the morning, warmed up the soup. Let me see. One gallon of water, one potato, and one onion, I think. Or maybe it was two potatoes and one onion, but never mind, it was warm. She was so friendly, we just enjoyed it. It doesn't have to be as good as the friendship. I wish that had spent the night there. Found it better. That's interesting because we found, Laura and I, that our friends, since we've moved here from Oregon, have pretty much been newcomers too. People that haven't been here for that long. I wonder if that's what you found too. They had a hard time with the old timers that was here.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And they saw that they needed a lot of education or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, the people that were new here?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They used to be here. They were just different from us, see.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Now when we went over on Central Ridge... We've been back in Canada and back again. Dad couldn't stand them here. He got awfully tired. He liked to get out of here. That's why he got up and went over to Central Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Your dad. No, it wasn't Daddy. Well, he said, I'd never make it here. He said, there's a new country, prairie land if you want it, or timber.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Or a little of each.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He said, I'm going over there. But you were starting to say that the people over here were... That the new people that came in saw the old people were kind of backwards.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's what you were starting to say. Well, yeah, now after they got out... Yeah, they got around. Got around with the... You were more worldly. You had more experience. Well, he'd been up in Canada 20 years and boy, I'd been around a lot. Not only that, but over there on Central Ridge we had all nationalities. We had French, we had Irish. Our first, closest neighbor was Irish, of course. And then, let's see, the Swede we could get along fine with, you know. Was three miles down there. On Central Ridge. But he didn't come for a few years. He didn't come right away. He bought a place at my uncle's homestead.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: He got that and that's how we got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When this guy was buying up all the land in here, did he try to buy you a place too?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Buying up the land here at this place? Oh, this place?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah, he wanted it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he offer you good money for it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No. Yeah, he offered me. He offered, but he offered the rest, but it wasn't too good. Well, at that time it was as good, as better than it had ever been offered. Yeah. But I said, it's not for sale. And I've been saying that all the time. And I've had, I don't know how many offers to sell, to buy. Well, you know. I don't want it. You know, this is something that I can remember back when I was just a little kid. And I used to enjoy, I used to enjoy coming over here. And you know, this house was built when I was still pretty young. And I remember this house more than I do anymore. I think it was the 1900s. Well, you know, I was only ten.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And we only made about one brick here. All these mixtures. Ten years from Central Ridge. We couldn't go right away. We had to get big enough for one thing, to ride a horse. Thirty -five miles horseback. And it had to be bareback. Because we couldn't find enough saddles. Well, Gee asked me if I could ride a bareback. Sure I can ride anything. I could ride a rail all the way over. So, I got it from this settler, this Swede. Yeah, he left me a settler. And it was safe. My gosh, it's thin as a rail. But it was alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You came over by yourself then?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, my brother and I. He was twelve and I was ten. And we knew, we thought we knew the way. And we found the way over. And you know, it was cut across a lot of places that you wouldn't know about now. When you crossed the river on the ferry. Across the river, what do you call it, the Clearwater into the ferry. When you got across, you went right up one of those drawers. Right up to the top of the hill. Well, any kind of rig you had to go around the Swiss banks and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that for me?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Sure it is. I want you to taste the Swiss mocha. Well, I know, but you got to have something more in your stomach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, did you come over here for special occasions like Thanksgiving or something like that? Were there special times when the family got together?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, the special time was when Dad could spare us.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: In the summertime. And we had, you know, forty acres of beans to hold and clean up. It's like a little bread and cheese. And five acres of orchard around the house. That looks like, that looks like breccia and stuff around the house. That's orchard. And there was no weeds supposed to be in there. And you had to clean everything. And for two boys, that isn't bad. And you keep all those beans and all that corn and stuff cleaned up. And sometimes we even rented from neighbors because they said we just can't take care of it. You take sixty acres or whatever you can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Take off of it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, that's on the outside of Dad's place. Besides what you did for him. Yeah. Yeah, well, what you get from Dad, it just depends on what he wants and thinks you need. If he thinks you need five dollars, he'll give it to you. But if you don't, he won't. And he'll want to know all about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it for?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You've got to answer all those questions. So for him, you were working pretty much for nothing. Yeah, all the time. You were the boy. And if you've made a walk outside and come back in, that check goes to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It does, huh?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah. Well, you could keep a little of it, you know, if you'd argue.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sounds like old country custom, doesn't it?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Sure, Dad. Well, Helmer had a lot of trouble with his boy, with his boy around, his boys and all these smart kids. He ran away. Helmer. Yeah, he told me about that. And he treated them different after that. Yeah, well, Helmer ran away. But Helmer tried to bring his kid up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The same way his father brought him up?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: I guess so. And his boy just found out that his dad ran away.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: And after that, his dad treated him better, so he ran away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he found out that Helmer ran away before he went away?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: There's any amount of guys that do better to tell you. Everybody tell. That's one thing. They always have something like that around them. Well, I'd tell him in a minute. I'd tell him in a minute. Well. He ran away to school. I'll tell him after the kid. The way it breaks him to death is to just leave him. Get out and I wouldn't tell him nothing. I'd say, well, yeah, he stopped by here and had dinner or something.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Said he was going someplace. I don't know what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what happened? Did his boy stop?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Did the boy stop by at his... No, but I'd say that's... It happened before we came here. He came, but some of the neighbors, I think, are mine. Well, that idea is a kind of funny one that you should do all that work for your parents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Doesn't it sound like your parents were kind of exploiting the kids?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: They used him in Canada to this American family. They had a horse trader. The kids worked for Steiner and then the old man was up there in his buggy ready to collect the wages. And getting the money from me right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I didn't think much of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ask your kids to work for that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you feel about your father making you do that? Was that fair?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I tell you the way I used to think. Now, I had... I farmed it one year. See, Dad says, well, that last year he says Helmer had it. Now he says I'm going to have Steiner's to go to red pick lakes. Well, I ran into him. He said, you know, he said you made... He was honest, one thing. He said, you got $750 coming. Now he says, you know, that's too much for a kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what would you do with all that money, Steve?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So he said, you know, I think $150 is all you need. You just go to school, see. Oh, boy. Well, I knew him. I said, yes, but I had an ocean teller I could work out and make. And that's the money. You can just keep the whole darn thing. But I was too afraid that he'd do it. You mean you only got... He picked me up on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You only got $150?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For $750 of money?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, yeah, that's what I... That was what he told me I had coming out of my years, for it, see. But you earned $750.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he figure that out? The crop you made or what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, I made a better crop maybe than my brother did before. Well, that's the price of the crop that he made. Well, $750 what you should have gotten after he got his rent. Yeah. Yeah, after he got his rent. And the debts have paid off. You made $750 clear then. Yeah. But he took $600 out of it. Yeah, he took... So he left me $150. But did you know that before he was going to... Oh, he told me. He told me about $150 was plenty instead of $750. He didn't tell him in the spring though when he was... He told you in the fall. Yeah. So there you are. Well, that's what I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you rent from him again after that? Hey? Did you rent from him again after that?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: No, that was my last year, you know.

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: That's your... I forget now how much it was, $100 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Riding ditches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Riding ditches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Riding ditches?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Well, that's what... Yeah, that's what we call it down there. This side, irrigation. You find you go to this place here and there's so much water to let out for this fellow and so much for the other guy. And you got to take a lot of boiling outs here and there and you got to be honest. Well, that don't seem... 22, you were no kid at that time. You could have used the $600. He told me that story and I said, well, boy, anybody that's dumb enough at that age to let that happen, those are the desserts. No, but this is all right. This is your dad. Doesn't make any difference. Yeah, I know, but here when he was in an accident and he thought maybe that he wouldn't pull through, he had the guy come out to make out his will and he said, well, he says, I'll tell you, I've got so many quarter seconds and I want the best quarter seconds for the two oldest boys, see, with the building zone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other, the rest of them can have what's left, see?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: So he favored us because he says they've done all the work around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that after or before you read it from him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah, this was one of his kids coming up. This one you were still young. Yeah, I'd say 17, 16 or 14, something like that. So you felt obligated to him. So you felt like he would do that, but that was, I mean, this was before he got married, see, and he didn't want to get married. He wanted some of us to get married and then stay there on the place. Well, I didn't like that idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not? Because you would have been running from him?

speaker: Jean Wilson Ringsage or Stiner Ringsage
words: You'd be running the place from him, you see. It wouldn't be your own place. No, it wouldn't be yours. And he might put up an argument like he did before, I don't know. Yeah, and so it might break up everything. The best way is to get out of home, get out on somebody else's place. I'd get along with anybody, any place I ever went.
